EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm produced moderate to heavy snow across much of central Pennsylvania. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches over the central mountains and Laurel Highlands to 15 to 25 inches across the lower Susquehanna valley. Near-blizzard conditions were common across most of the area as gusty winds combined with blowing and falling snow to produce very low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulation ranged between 15 to 20 inches.

A wintry mix of mainly freezing rain occurred during the second half of the day on the 17th over the Pocono Plateau. Ice accretions averaged one quarter to one half an inch with the highest amounts in Monroe County. Snowfall and sleet were generally less than half an inch. Freezing rain began around Noon EST on the 17th. As precipitation intensity increased, the freezing rain briefly changed over to sleet and snow during the first half of the afternoon. Strong and gusty west to northwest winds occurred for nearly twenty-four hours across Eastern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph, except over the higher terrain of the Poconos where they nearly reached 60 mph. The strong winds downed weak trees, tree limbs and power lines and resulted in widely scattered power outages. In the Poconos, about 1,000 homes and businesses lost power. The strong to high winds also caused drifting snow and resulted in slippery travel and accidents. About 1,000 PECO Energy customers in the local Philadelphia area also lost power.||Peak wind gusts included 59 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 49 mph in West Grove (Chester County) and 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Reading (Berks County).||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intense low pressure system that retrograded from the Nova Scotia coastal waters to the Gulf of Maine on the 2nd and 3rd and a strong high pressure system located over the northern plains. The strongest winds occurred as the low pressure system continued its retrogression west through Nova Scotia on the evening of the 2nd and persisted until the low started to weaken and move to the east again, on the 3rd.

A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred across Upper Bucks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos during the morning and afternoon on Thanksgiving Day (the 25th). Accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches, mainly on grassy surfaces with ice accretions of one to two tenths mainly in the higher terrain of the Poconos.||Precipitation spread into the area in the form of snow during the morning. Once the precipitation intensity weakened, the snow changed to rain in the Lehigh Valley and Upper Bucks County around Noon EST. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet and freezing rain. Enough warm air moved into the valleys to change the freezing rain to rain in the Poconos, but freezing rain persisted over the higher terrain until all of it ended around 6 p.m. EST that evening. Because the recent weather was warm and the precipitation occurred during the day, most of the ice accretions were confined to exposed surfaces and bridges and overpasses. Roadways in contact with the ground remained wet. Some slushy accumulations occurred on roadways while it was snowing.||Specific snow accumulations included 2.3 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 1.8 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.2 inches in Upper Black Eddy (Bucks County) and 1.0 inch in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry mix was caused by a warm front approaching the Middle Atlantic States from the Ohio Valley. A high pressures system over southeast Canada provided a fresh supply of cold, dry air that made it possible for a wintry mix to occur until warmer air moved into the state both at the surface and aloft during the afternoon of the 25th.

Accumulating snow showers that coincided with the morning commute on the 28th led to numerous accidents from the Philadelphia northern suburbs through the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The snow spread across the area just before the start of the morning commute between 4 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and tapered off or ended between 9 a.m. and 10 a.m. EST. Accumulations were only 1 to 3 inches, but untreated roadways became slippery quickly.||In Bucks County, a seven vehicle accident occurred in the southbound lanes of the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension near Exit 44 near Quakertown. In Lehigh County, a truck overturned on westbound Interstate 78 between the Pennsylvania State Route 100 and New Smithville Road exits. Several accidents occurred in both directions on U.S. Route 22 in Allentown. In Northampton County, a truck overturned into a cornfield in Lower Mount Bethel Township and closed the roadway.||Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.4 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.5 inches in Chapman (Carbon County), 1.0 inch in Quakertown (Bucks County), Bechtelsville (Berks County), Nazareth and Forks Township (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.5 inches in Allentown (Lehigh County).||The snow showers were triggered by an approaching cold front that moved through the eastern part of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon.

A strong cold front moved east across northeastern Pennsylvania late on December 31st.  Cold north to northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop early on January 1st.  Intermittent snow showers were then reported for several days.  This activity finally diminished during the afternoon and evening hours of January 5th.  Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour with the most intense bands.  Drier air finally began to move into the area on January 5th causing the snow showers to finally dissipate late in the day.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and to 35 mph on the 3rd.  These winds combined with the snow caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Snowfall amounts varied considerably across Erie and Crawford Counties.  Totals ranged from around a foot near Lake Erie to nearly five feet in northern Crawford County.  Peak totals in Erie County were over four feet during this event.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and hundreds of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began in Crawford County during the morning of January 1st.  Moderate to heavy snow showers then continued through the afternoon hours of the 4th.  From the evening of the 4th through the evening of the 5th the snow shower activity was very scattered with most areas seeing only occasional light snow or flurries.  Some of the heaviest snow fell during the afternoon of the 3rd with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour at times.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting along with visibilities near zero at times.  Snowfall totals across Crawford County varied considerably during this five day event with the greatest amounts reported across the northern and central portions of the county.  A peak total from the 1st through the 5th of 59.2 inches was measured southwest of Conneautville.  Most of that snow fell on the 2nd and 3rd.  Significantly less snow was reported in southern Crawford County with Titusville in the southeast corner of the county seeing only 14.5 inches during this event.  Other five day totals across the county included:  51.1 inches northwest of Crossingville and 46.5 inches near Meadville.  Drifts as much as 6 feet tall were reported in the county.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm produced moderate to heavy snow across much of central Pennsylvania. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches over the central mountains and Laurel Highlands to 15 to 25 inches across the lower Susquehanna valley. Near-blizzard conditions were common across most of the area as gusty winds combined with blowing and falling snow to produce very low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulation ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong northwest winds, an arctic air mass and temperatures in the single numbers produced wind chill factors of around fifteen degrees below zero during the first part of the day of the 3rd on the Pocono Plateau. At Mount Pocono (Monroe County), the lowest hourly wind chill factor was seventeen degrees below zero and the low temperature the morning of the 3rd was 7 degrees above zero.||The low wind chill factor was caused by a retrograding low pressure system in the Gulf of Maine that not only prolonged the strong winds, but also helped bring arctic air into the region from Canada.

Accumulating snow showers that coincided with the morning commute on the 28th led to numerous accidents from the Philadelphia northern suburbs through the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The snow spread across the area just before the start of the morning commute between 4 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and tapered off or ended between 9 a.m. and 10 a.m. EST. Accumulations were only 1 to 3 inches, but untreated roadways became slippery quickly.||In Bucks County, a seven vehicle accident occurred in the southbound lanes of the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension near Exit 44 near Quakertown. In Lehigh County, a truck overturned on westbound Interstate 78 between the Pennsylvania State Route 100 and New Smithville Road exits. Several accidents occurred in both directions on U.S. Route 22 in Allentown. In Northampton County, a truck overturned into a cornfield in Lower Mount Bethel Township and closed the roadway.||Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.4 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.5 inches in Chapman (Carbon County), 1.0 inch in Quakertown (Bucks County), Bechtelsville (Berks County), Nazareth and Forks Township (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.5 inches in Allentown (Lehigh County).||The snow showers were triggered by an approaching cold front that moved through the eastern part of Pennsylvania during the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper type low pressure system caused light snow to fall for around eight hours around the Greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area during the first part of the day on the 8th. Accumulations averaged around an inch and fell mainly between Midnight EST and 8 a.m. EST on the 8th. Similar accumulations occurred in the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos. The early arrival during the day and recent cold weather made untreated roadways slippery for the morning commute. Actual snowfall included exactly 1.0 inch at the Philadelphia International Airport. ||The clipper low pressure system moved from Illinois on the morning of the 7th, to Ohio during the early evening on the 7th, to the Poconos at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th and well off the New England Coast by 7 p.m. EST that evening. The snow ended shortly after the clipper's occluded and cold fronts moved through Eastern Pennsylvania.

The strong and persistent offshore flow coupled with the recent full moon caused blow out tides at the times of low tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries from the evening of the 2nd into the overnight of the 3rd. The lowest low tides occurred with the evening low tide on the 3rd. That low tide in Philadelphia was 3.06 feet below mean lower low water. Low water problems start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.||The blowout tides were caused by an intense low pressure system that retrograded from the Nova Scotia coastal waters on the afternoon of the 2nd, across Nova Scotia on the evening of the 2nd, into the Gulf of Maine on the morning and afternoon of the 3rd before it started moving east again that evening.

A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred across Upper Bucks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos during the morning and afternoon on Thanksgiving Day (the 25th). Accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches, mainly on grassy surfaces with ice accretions of one to two tenths mainly in the higher terrain of the Poconos.||Precipitation spread into the area in the form of snow during the morning. Once the precipitation intensity weakened, the snow changed to rain in the Lehigh Valley and Upper Bucks County around Noon EST. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet and freezing rain. Enough warm air moved into the valleys to change the freezing rain to rain in the Poconos, but freezing rain persisted over the higher terrain until all of it ended around 6 p.m. EST that evening. Because the recent weather was warm and the precipitation occurred during the day, most of the ice accretions were confined to exposed surfaces and bridges and overpasses. Roadways in contact with the ground remained wet. Some slushy accumulations occurred on roadways while it was snowing.||Specific snow accumulations included 2.3 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 1.8 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.2 inches in Upper Black Eddy (Bucks County) and 1.0 inch in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry mix was caused by a warm front approaching the Middle Atlantic States from the Ohio Valley. A high pressures system over southeast Canada provided a fresh supply of cold, dry air that made it possible for a wintry mix to occur until warmer air moved into the state both at the surface and aloft during the afternoon of the 25th.

A strong cold front moved east across northeastern Pennsylvania late on December 31st.  Cold north to northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop early on January 1st.  Intermittent snow showers were then reported for several days.  This activity finally diminished during the afternoon and evening hours of January 5th.  Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour with the most intense bands.  Drier air finally began to move into the area on January 5th causing the snow showers to finally dissipate late in the day.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and to 35 mph on the 3rd.  These winds combined with the snow caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Snowfall amounts varied considerably across Erie and Crawford Counties.  Totals ranged from around a foot near Lake Erie to nearly five feet in northern Crawford County.  Peak totals in Erie County were over four feet during this event.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and hundreds of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began in Erie County during the morning of January 1st.  Moderate to heavy snow showers then continued through the afternoon hours of the 4th.  From the evening of the 4th through midday on the 5th the snow shower activity was very scattered with most areas seeing only occasional light snow or flurries.  Some of the heaviest snow fell during the afternoon of the 3rd with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour at times.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting along with visibilities near zero at times.  Snowfall totals across inland Erie County during this five day event were generally between two and three feet.  A peak total from the 1st through the 5th of 43.5 inches was measured at Edinboro.  Most of that snow fell on the 2nd and 3rd.  Other five day totals across the county included:  42.4 inches in Amity Township; 37.0 inches south of North East and 33.5 inches at Pont.  Drifts as much as 6 feet tall were reported in the county.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light accumulating snow fell across extreme southeastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon into the middle of the evening on the 30th. The region was along the northwest edge of the accumulating snow. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery. Representative snowfall included 2.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.6 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County) and 1.3 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County). The snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved along the southern Gulf Coast states from the morning of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The low reformed over the Carolina coastal waters later in the day on the 30th and moved quickly northeast that evening and overnight. The track was too far to the southeast for the heavier snow, which fell to the south, to reach into Pennsylvania.

A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

Double barrel low pressure systems brought a light accumulating snow to Eastern Pennsylvania on the evening and overnight on the 2nd. Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches. Snow started during the early evening of the 2nd and ended by 5 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Since the snow held off until after the evening commute was done and stopped before the morning commute started, the number of reported accidents was low.| |Representative snowfall included 3.5 inches in Morgantown (Berks County), 3.3 inches in Downingtown (Chester County), 2.8 inches in Boyertown (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Montgomeryville (Montgomery County), 2.4 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 2.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.0 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County), 1.9 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 1.7 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 1.6 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.5 inches in West Chester (Chester County) and Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 1.4 inches in Morrisville (Bucks County), 1.0 inch in Palmerton (Carbon County), 0.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 0.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 0.5 inches at Pocono Summit (Monroe County).| |The snow was caused by a pair of low pressure systems: one that was moving through the southern Great Lakes and another that was forming off the Georgia coast on the morning of the 2nd. The latter low pressure system intensified as it moved northeast off the southeastern coast overnight on the 2nd while the former dissipated.

A major winter storm dropped 20 to 30 inches of snow from Philadelphia south in Eastern Pennsylvania, 10 to 20 inches across the northern Philadelphia suburbs, 6 to 12 inches across Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and less than 6 inches in the Poconos from  the late afternoon of the 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. The 28.5 inches of snow that fell in Philadelphia became the second highest single snowfall event on history, only surpassed by the 30.7 inches of snow that fell during the Blizzard of January 1996.||Snow spread from south to north across Eastern Pennsylvania from late in the afternoon on the 5th into the middle of the evening of the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times during the first half of the day on the 6th across southeast Pennsylvania and Berks County. The snow ended during the morning of the 6th in the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos and during the afternoon of the 6th in southeast Pennsylvania. ||Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell declared a statewide disaster emergency. Many county and municipalities declared their own snow emergencies. National Guard troops were deployed to assist the State Police. Many roads and highways in southeastern Pennsylvania were impassable. Speed limits were reduced on the Delaware River bridges. Amtrak canceled train service. The SEPTA regional rail and bus lines suspended service on the 6th in and around Philadelphia. Airlines canceled flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport on the 6th. They resumed on the 7th. Many businesses, stores and malls were closed on the 6th. Events were canceled or postponed. Philadelphia schools were closed on Monday the 8th. Where the snow was relatively lighter, numerous accidents and fender benders were reported in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. In Berks County, several suspected heart attacks from shoveling snow occurred. A head-on collision in Cumru Township caused an injury. In West Lawn, a pedestrian was injured after he was struck by a vehicle and tossed from Westbound U.S. Route 422 onto Penn Avenue.  The snow was relatively fluffy and caused very few power outages. For the second time within a week a major winter storm affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 10th and 11th and smashed seasonal snowfall records in the Philadelphia area. Snowfall averaged one to two feet with some locally higher amounts in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. ||Around the local Philadelphia area, snow began during the early evening on the 9th. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 10th. Surface temperatures responded slower and the sleet and freezing rain changed over to rain during the morning of the 10th. Some sleet mixed in from time to time. As the low pressure system moved northeast of the region, the rain changed back to snow by Noon EST on the 10th and fell heavy at times during the afternoon. Winds also increased and started to down snow laden tree limbs and trees. The snow ended late in the evening on the 10th. In the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania, snow began during the evening of the 9th and fell at its heaviest from the late morning into the afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended during the evening of the 10th.||Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell declared a state of disaster emergency. Many counties, cities and municipalities issued their own snow and/or states of emergencies. Even Pennsylvania Power and Light declared its own snow emergency. Many city, federal, social and county offices as well as courthouses were closed on the 10th and 11th. Many municipal meetings were postponed. Interstates 76, 176, 476, 676 and 78 were all closed on the afternoon of the 10th and reopened on the 11th. The Pennsylvania National Guard was activated to assist stranded motorists and to assist people without power in southeastern Pennsylvania with food and blankets. Schools were closed on the 10th and 11th, some even on the 12th. Many businesses were closed on the 10th. Philadelphia International Airport was closed on the 10th and reopened on the afternoon of the 11th. SEPTA regional rail and bus service was suspended in southeastern Pennsylvania on the 10th and 11th. Because the heavy snow clung to the trees first and then the winds increased, PECO Energy suffered its worst winter time power outage (9th highest overall) since the ice storm of January 1994. About 225,000 homes and businesses lost power. Several shelters were opened in the Philadelphia suburbs. Power was not fully restored until the 13th. About 8,500 Pennsylvania Power and light customers lost power, their power was fully restored by the 11th.  Many school districts ran out of snow days and municipal snow removal budgets were exceeded. Trash collections were postponed. The combination of the two heavy snow events within a week started causing roof collapses in the Philadelphia suburbs. No injuries occurred.||The combination of heavy snow and then the increasing wind caused many pine trees to snap and/or be uprooted in and around Philadelphia. It was the 9th highest power outage in PECO Energy's history. They had 1,549 outage events and about 225,000 customers affected. Outages by county were 60,500 in Chester, 55,000 in Delaware, 47,650 Bucks, 44,700 in Montgomery and 17,000 in Philadelphia. All power was restored by the 13th. The utility replaced 56 miles of cable, 50 transformers, 51 poles, replaced or reconnected 8,000 fuses, and 1,550 pole top cross arms. Damage to the infrastructure was estimated at 15 million dollars.||In Philadelphia, a 65-year-old man and a 66-year-old man died from heart attacks shoveling snow. Schools remained closed through the 12th. In Bucks County, the mid section (Middletown, Northampton, Yardley, Bristol, Upper and Lower Makefield) of the county was hardest hit by the power outages. There were 220 separate outages. Three shelters were opened.  A tin and wood storage building in Middletown collapsed. In Montgomery County, the outages were centered around Lower Merion, Abington, Cheltenham, Upper and Lower Moreland, Upper Dublin and Ambler. In Pottstown, the roof of a modular building collapsed. Porches collapsed in Narberth and Royersford. In Springfield Township, an ice skating rink was evacuated after the roof's trusses started to pop. In Chester County, a storage building collapsed in West Chester, a Farmer's Market Building collapsed in Caln Township.   ||In Berks County, two separate tractor-trailer jackknifes initiated the closure of Interstate 78. This also led to the chain reaction closing of Interstates 176, 76, 476 and 676 in southeastern Pennsylvania. About 200 motorists and 180 tractor-trailers were stranded for up to 12 hours on a five mile stretch of Interstate 78 as large drifts occurred. While most of the interstates were opened on the morning of the 11th, westbound I78 was not reopened until that afternoon. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County, 2,100 in Boyerstown and 900 in Reading. Power was restored on the 11th. Ironically in Spring Township, the roof of a commercial building collapsed and damaged twenty snow throwers. In Boyertown, the entire roof of a two story commercial building collapsed on East Philadelphia Avenue. In Lower Alsace Township, the roof of a two car garage collapsed. In Reading, the third floor of a large brick building on Buttonwood Street collapsed. Several canopies, awnings and overhangs also collapsed. In the Lehigh Valley, about 4,500 homes and businesses lost power in Bethlehem, Upper Saucon, Coopersburg, Easton and Bangor. A porch collapsed in Emmaus (Northampton County).   ||Representative snowfall included 26.8 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 26.1 inches in South Coventry (Chester County), 23.3 inches in Boyertown (Berks County), 23.0 inches in Eagleville (Montgomery County), 21.0 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 20.0 inches in Reading (Berks County), Collegeville (Montgomery County) and Boothwyn (Delaware County), 19.5 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia County), 19.3 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 19.0 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 18.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Caln (Chester County), 17.8 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 17.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 17.1 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 17.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), Blue Bell (Montgomery County) and Pine Valley (Philadelphia County), 16.5 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 16.1 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 15.8 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 15.6 inches in Easton (Northampton County), 15.1 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 15.0 inches in Emmaus (Lehigh County), 14.0 inches in Honey Brook (Chester County), 13.9 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County), 11.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and 9.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County). ||Only 10 days into February and the 44.9 inches of snow that already fallen within the month at the Philadelphia International Airport established a new snowfall record for February. It moved northeast and reached the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 9th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 9th, it was located near Charleston, South Carolina. It then moved northnortheast and was near Norfolk, Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 10th, Georgetown, Delaware at 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, Atlantic City, New Jersey at 10 a.m. EST on the 10th and just east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at 4 p.m. EST on the 10th. The low pressure system then drifted slowly east the rest of the afternoon into the overnight of the 10th. The heaviest snow occurred just to the north and west of this changeover in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs.

There were 220 separate outages. Three shelters were opened.  A tin and wood storage building in Middletown collapsed. In Montgomery County, the outages were centered around Lower Merion, Abington, Cheltenham, Upper and Lower Moreland, Upper Dublin and Ambler. In Pottstown, the roof of a modular building collapsed. Porches collapsed in Narberth and Royersford. In Springfield Township, an ice skating rink was evacuated after the roof's trusses started to pop. In Chester County, a storage building collapsed in West Chester, a Farmer's Market Building collapsed in Caln Township.   ||In Berks County, two separate tractor-trailer jackknifes initiated the closure of Interstate 78. While most of the interstates were opened on the morning of the 11th, westbound I78 was not reopened until that afternoon. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County, 2,100 in Boyerstown and 900 in Reading. Power was restored on the 11th. Ironically in Spring Township, the roof of a commercial building collapsed and damaged twenty snow throwers. In Boyertown, the entire roof of a two story commercial building collapsed on East Philadelphia Avenue. In Lower Alsace Township, the roof of a two car garage collapsed. A double shot of wintry precipitation affected Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the evening of the 15th and again during the daytime hours of the 16th. Total snowfall from both events averaged one to three inches. The first shot of precipitation started as snow late in the afternoon of the 15th. It changed over to freezing rain around the local Philadelphia area just before it ended. Precipitation ended by midnight EST on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall with the first band affected lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The second shot of precipitation fell as snow and lasted for about five hours during the daytime on the 16th. Around a half inch to inch of additional snow fell. Untreated roadways were slippery, especially during the first precipitation shot as it fell at night. ||Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 3.2 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.7 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), Rockledge (Philadelphia County) and Elkins Park (Montgomery County) , 1.8 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 1.5 inches at the Franklin Institute (Philadelphia County) and Harleysville (Montgomery County), 1.3 inches art the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  ||The double shot of wintry precipitation was caused by a low pressure system and its upper level support. The low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 15th northeast into western Pennsylvania during the early evening of the 15th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of its occluded, warm and cold fronts in Virginia early that evening. This low pressure system moved northeast and was off the New Jersey coast shortly after Midnight EST on the 16th ending the first shot of precipitation. The second shot of precipitation occurred as an upper level low moved across the southeast part of the state during the daytime hours on the 16th.

The heaviest snowfall with the first band affected lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The second shot of precipitation fell as snow and lasted for about five hours during the daytime on the 16th. Around a half inch to inch of additional snow fell. Untreated roadways were slippery, especially during the first precipitation shot as it fell at night. ||Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 3.2 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.7 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), Rockledge (Philadelphia County) and Elkins Park (Montgomery County) , 1.8 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 1.5 inches at the Franklin Institute (Philadelphia County) and Harleysville (Montgomery County), 1.3 inches art the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  ||The double shot of wintry precipitation was caused by a low pressure system and its upper level support. The low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 15th northeast into western Pennsylvania during the early evening of the 15th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of its occluded, warm and cold fronts in Virginia early that evening. This low pressure system moved northeast and was off the New Jersey coast shortly after Midnight EST on the 16th ending the first shot of precipitation. The second shot of precipitation occurred as an upper level low moved across the southeast part of the state during the daytime hours on the 16th.

A major winter storm dropped 20 to 30 inches of snow from Philadelphia south in Eastern Pennsylvania, 10 to 20 inches across the northern Philadelphia suburbs, 6 to 12 inches across Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and less than 6 inches in the Poconos from  the late afternoon of the 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. The 28.5 inches of snow that fell in Philadelphia became the second highest single snowfall event on history, only surpassed by the 30.7 inches of snow that fell during the Blizzard of January 1996.||Snow spread from south to north across Eastern Pennsylvania from late in the afternoon on the 5th into the middle of the evening of the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times during the first half of the day on the 6th across southeast Pennsylvania and Berks County. The snow ended during the morning of the 6th in the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos and during the afternoon of the 6th in southeast Pennsylvania. ||Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell declared a statewide disaster emergency. Many county and municipalities declared their own snow emergencies. National Guard troops were deployed to assist the State Police. Many roads and highways in southeastern Pennsylvania were impassable. Speed limits were reduced on the Delaware River bridges. Amtrak canceled train service. The SEPTA regional rail and bus lines suspended service on the 6th in and around Philadelphia. Airlines canceled flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport on the 6th. They resumed on the 7th. Many businesses, stores and malls were closed on the 6th. Events were canceled or postponed. Philadelphia schools were closed on Monday the 8th. Where the snow was relatively lighter, numerous accidents and fender benders were reported in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. In Berks County, several suspected heart attacks from shoveling snow occurred. A head-on collision in Cumru Township caused an injury. In West Lawn, a pedestrian was injured after he was struck by a vehicle and tossed from Westbound U.S. Route 422 onto Penn Avenue.  The snow was relatively fluffy and caused very few power outages. PECO Energy reported only 200 homes and businesses lost power in Bucks and Montgomery Counties.||Representative snowfall included 29.5 inches in Folcroft and Newtown Square (Delaware County), 28.5 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 25.5 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 23.0 inches in New London (Chester County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 22.0 inches in the Roxborough (Philadelphia County), 20.0 inches in Norristown (Montgomery County), 18.3 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 15.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 11.0 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 10.5 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 7.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 4.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 1.0 inch in Pocono Summit (Monroe County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east across northern Mexico and emerged in the Gulf of Mexico near Brownsville, Texas on the morning of the 4th. The low pressure system moved east and was located near New Orleans, Louisiana at 7 p.m. EST on the 4th and Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. The low pressure system then started to move northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th; Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 1 a.m. EST on the 6th; the Virginia coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 6th and then drifted slowly east off the lower Delmarva Peninsula the rest of the day on the 6th. The storm track was perfect for heavy snow in southeastern Pennsylvania, but became less favorable (too far southeast) for places farther to the north.

A slow-moving low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic states brought heavy rain to central Pennsylvania. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across southern Pennsylvania. The heavy rainfall combined with snow melt resulted in widespread minor to moderate river and areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads and low-lying areas were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 1 and 3 inches caused areal flooding at the intersection of Licking Creek, Tuscarora Creek and the Main Stem Juniata River. The flooding was mainly confined to the Port Royal area. The Tuscarora Creek was out of its banks and crested near 14 feet or about 4 feet above flood stage. The flooding closed several secondary roads including Sandbar Road, Locust Grove Road and River Road into Juniata Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic states brought heavy rain to central Pennsylvania. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across southern Pennsylvania. The heavy rainfall combined with snow melt resulted in widespread minor to moderate river and areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads and low-lying areas were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 1 and 2.5 inches combined with snow melt to produce extensive areal and river flooding. The flooding occurred along the Main Stem and Little Juniata River, Stone Creek, Spruce Creek and Aughwick Creek. Approximately 20 secondary roads were closed in Miller, Cromwell, Juniata, Shirley, Smithfield, Spruce Creek, Porter, Logan and Todd Townships. Flooding was also reported in Warriors Mark, Huntingdon and Mill Creek Boroughs. The Little Juniata River River at Spruce Creek crested near 10 feet or about 2 feet above flood stage early in the morning on the 14th. The Aughwick Creek at Shirleysburg crested near 13 feet or about 3 feet above flood stage during the mid-morning on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third widespread major winter storm of the month of February affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the early morning of the 25th into the middle of the day on the 26th. In the Poconos, snowfall averaged 12 to 24 inches. In the Lehigh Valley, snowfall averaged around one foot and in southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia 6 to 12 inches. The lightest snowfall occurred in Berks County. Snow began falling before dawn on the 25th. In and around Philadelphia, precipitation initially started as rain before changing to snow by 7 a.m. EST. Except for the Poconos and parts of the Lehigh Valley, the snow that fell during the day on the 25th had a hard time accumulating on roadways that mainly stayed wet. Once the sun set, both the snowfall intensity and wind increased. But unlike the previous winter storm, the wind increased first and then the snow became heavier. Thus less power outages occurred. The snow fell heavy at times overnight on the 25th and decreased in intensity during the day on the 26th. The snow ended around Noon EST, except lingered into the afternoon in the Poconos. The strongest winds occurred from 6 p.m. EST on the 25th and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th. ||Schools were closed on the 25th in Philadelphia. Most schools in Eastern Pennsylvania were closed on the 26th. Municipal meetings were canceled as were sports games. Hundreds of flights were canceled going in and out of Philadelphia International Airport. Snow drifts forced the closure of Pennsylvania State Route 10 in Chester County. Because the winds increased before the heavier snow, PECO Energy reported less than 1,000 of its customers lost power in Southeastern Pennsylvania. Only a few trees were knocked down. ||Representative snowfall included 20.5 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 15.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 12.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 12.0 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 11.7 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 11.2 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 10.5 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 10.3 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 10.1 inches in Exton (Chester County), 10.0 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), 9.4 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 8.0 inches in Sellersville (Bucks County), Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Downingtown (Chester County), 7.5 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 7.1 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia County), 6.6 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 6.0 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 5.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 3.9 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County) and 2.2 inches in West Lawn (Berks County).||Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Coatesville (Chester County), 43 mph at Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 40 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 39 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 38 mph in Reading (Berks County). ||This last winter storm concluded a record breaking and unprecedented month for snow in February across Eastern Pennsylvania, especially in Philadelphia. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the 51.5 inches of snow that fell in February 2010 surpassed the previous snowiest February by 20 inches and was the snowiest month on record. The 51.5 inches of snow that fell In February alone would qualify it as the 5th snowiest winter on record. In all, 78.7 inches of snow fell this season in Philadelphia and was the snowiest season on record. The previous record was 65.5 inches set in 1995-6. At the Lehigh Valley International Airport, the 42.9 inches of snow that fell during February 2010 was the snowiest February on record, but the second snowiest month on record, barely surpassed by January 1925 (43.2 inches). The seasonal snowfall total of 59.8 inches was the 6th snowiest season on record.||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed east of Florida on the 24th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 25th, it (992 millibar low pressure system) was well east of the North Carolina coast and was intensifying rapidly as it was moving north. At 1 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 987 millibar low well east of the Delmarva Peninsula. At 7 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 978 millibar low just east of Long Island. At 10 p.m. EST on the 25th, it deepened to 972 millibars and was located along the Rhode Island and Massachusetts border. From there, a rare occurrence happened as the low pressure system retrograded or move westward. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the 972 millibar low was in Connecticut; at 7 a.m. EST it weakened to 979 millibars and was over New York City. It spent the rest of the daylight hours weakening over the New York City area and then drifted slowly north that night. The heaviest snow across a widespread area occurred during the retrogression process, but the intensity of the snow decreased once the low pressure system weakened.

The latter low pressure system intensified as it moved northeast off the southeastern coast overnight on the 2nd while the former dissipated.

The storm track was perfect for heavy snow in southeastern Pennsylvania, but became less favorable (too far southeast) for places farther to the north.

A double shot of wintry precipitation affected Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the evening of the 15th and again during the daytime hours of the 16th. Total snowfall from both events averaged one to three inches. The first shot of precipitation started as snow late in the afternoon of the 15th. It changed over to freezing rain around the local Philadelphia area just before it ended. Precipitation ended by midnight EST on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall with the first band affected lower Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The second shot of precipitation fell as snow and lasted for about five hours during the daytime on the 16th. Around a half inch to inch of additional snow fell. Untreated roadways were slippery, especially during the first precipitation shot as it fell at night. ||Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 3.2 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.7 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), Rockledge (Philadelphia County) and Elkins Park (Montgomery County) , 1.8 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 1.5 inches at the Franklin Institute (Philadelphia County) and Harleysville (Montgomery County), 1.3 inches art the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  ||The double shot of wintry precipitation was caused by a low pressure system and its upper level support. The low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 15th northeast into western Pennsylvania during the early evening of the 15th. A new low pressure system formed on the triple point of its occluded, warm and cold fronts in Virginia early that evening. This low pressure system moved northeast and was off the New Jersey coast shortly after Midnight EST on the 16th ending the first shot of precipitation. The second shot of precipitation occurred as an upper level low moved across the southeast part of the state during the daytime hours on the 16th.

A protracted winter storm dropped a wintry mix of snow. sleet and freezing rain from the Lehigh Valley into the Poconos and affected Monroe County the most. The wintry conditions led to numerous accidents including a fatal one in Carbon County. Snow spread north from the Lehigh Valley into the Poconos during the evening of the 22nd. While warmer air at the surface and aloft changed the snow to rain in the Lehigh Valley very early on the 23rd, the cold air at the surface remained entrenched in the Poconos. A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred from around 4 a.m. EST until the middle of the afternoon in the Poconos before it changed back to all snow. The snow ended very early in the day on the 24th. ||A 35-year-old woman passenger and her 10-year-old son were killed on the afternoon of the 23rd after the Sports Utility Vehicle they were riding in on Interstate 80 in Kidder Township (Carbon County) went off the roadway, rolled over and struck a tree on the passenger side. The male driver was seriously injured and two other children suffered lesser injuries. On the night of the 23rd state police directed traffic off of Westbound Interstate 80 to Pennsylvania State Routes 611 and 940 and Interstate 380 because of poor visibilities. ||Representative snowfall included 8.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 8.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 7.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.8 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 2.0 inches in Easton (Northampton County), 1.5 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 0.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Ice accretions in the Poconos were less than a quarter of an inch.||The winter weather was caused by a double barrel low pressure system that were over Ohio and the Tennessee Valleys on the morning of the 22nd. They moved east into Lake Erie and the North Carolina coast respectfully during the early evening of the 22nd. By 7 a.m. EST on the 23rd, the Lake Erie low pressure system weakened and meandered around Lake Ontario. The North Carolina coastal low pressure system slowly drifted northeast and at 7 a.m. EST on the 23rd was located over the North Carolina coastal waters, 7 p.m. EST off the Virginia coastal waters and at 7 a.m. EST on the 24th was hundreds of miles east of the Virginia coast. An inverted trof that emanated northwest into the region from this low pressure system helped precipitation persist into the night of the 23rd.

A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

A slow-moving low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic states brought heavy rain to central Pennsylvania. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across southern Pennsylvania. The heavy rainfall combined with snow melt resulted in widespread minor to moderate river and areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads and low-lying areas were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 1 and 4 inches combined with snow melt to produce areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads were closed in the Upper Strasburg area. Flooding caused the evacuation of two mobile home parks in Greene Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic states brought heavy rain to central Pennsylvania. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across southern Pennsylvania. The heavy rainfall combined with snow melt resulted in widespread minor to moderate river and areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads and low-lying areas were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in excess of two inches combined with residual snow melt to produce widespread areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads were closed as a result of the flooding, especially in and around the towns of Altoona, Tyrone, Williamsburg and Bellwood.

A slow-moving low pressure system over the Mid-Atlantic states brought heavy rain to central Pennsylvania. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across southern Pennsylvania. The heavy rainfall combined with snow melt resulted in widespread minor to moderate river and areal flooding. Numerous secondary roads and low-lying areas were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 1 and 3 inches combined with melt water from a deep snow pack to produce extensive areal flooding along the Casselman and Youghiogheny Rivers. Considerable flooding also occurred along Stony Creek. The flooding evacuated an unknown number of Benson Borough residents. Numerous secondary roads were closed across the southern half of the county. Three personal care homes were evacuated due to high water covering access roads, displacing approximately 45 to 50 people. The Youghiogheny River at Confluence crested over 13 feet or 1.5 feet above flood stage. The county declared a disaster emergency for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third widespread major winter storm of the month of February affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the early morning of the 25th into the middle of the day on the 26th. In the Poconos, snowfall averaged 12 to 24 inches. In the Lehigh Valley, snowfall averaged around one foot and in southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia 6 to 12 inches. The lightest snowfall occurred in Berks County. Snow began falling before dawn on the 25th. In and around Philadelphia, precipitation initially started as rain before changing to snow by 7 a.m. EST. Except for the Poconos and parts of the Lehigh Valley, the snow that fell during the day on the 25th had a hard time accumulating on roadways that mainly stayed wet. Once the sun set, both the snowfall intensity and wind increased. But unlike the previous winter storm, the wind increased first and then the snow became heavier. Thus less power outages occurred. The snow fell heavy at times overnight on the 25th and decreased in intensity during the day on the 26th. The snow ended around Noon EST, except lingered into the afternoon in the Poconos. The strongest winds occurred from 6 p.m. EST on the 25th and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th. ||Schools were closed on the 25th in Philadelphia. Most schools in Eastern Pennsylvania were closed on the 26th. Municipal meetings were canceled as were sports games. Hundreds of flights were canceled going in and out of Philadelphia International Airport. Snow drifts forced the closure of Pennsylvania State Route 10 in Chester County. Because the winds increased before the heavier snow, PECO Energy reported less than 1,000 of its customers lost power in Southeastern Pennsylvania. Only a few trees were knocked down. ||Representative snowfall included 20.5 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 15.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 12.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 12.0 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 11.7 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 11.2 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 10.5 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 10.3 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 10.1 inches in Exton (Chester County), 10.0 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), 9.4 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 8.0 inches in Sellersville (Bucks County), Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Downingtown (Chester County), 7.5 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 7.1 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia County), 6.6 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 6.0 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 5.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 3.9 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County) and 2.2 inches in West Lawn (Berks County).||Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Coatesville (Chester County), 43 mph at Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 40 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 39 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 38 mph in Reading (Berks County). ||This last winter storm concluded a record breaking and unprecedented month for snow in February across Eastern Pennsylvania, especially in Philadelphia. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the 51.5 inches of snow that fell in February 2010 surpassed the previous snowiest February by 20 inches and was the snowiest month on record. The 51.5 inches of snow that fell In February alone would qualify it as the 5th snowiest winter on record. In all, 78.7 inches of snow fell this season in Philadelphia and was the snowiest season on record. The previous record was 65.5 inches set in 1995-6. At the Lehigh Valley International Airport, the 42.9 inches of snow that fell during February 2010 was the snowiest February on record, but the second snowiest month on record, barely surpassed by January 1925 (43.2 inches). The seasonal snowfall total of 59.8 inches was the 6th snowiest season on record.||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed east of Florida on the 24th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 25th, it (992 millibar low pressure system) was well east of the North Carolina coast and was intensifying rapidly as it was moving north. At 1 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 987 millibar low well east of the Delmarva Peninsula. At 7 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 978 millibar low just east of Long Island. At 10 p.m. EST on the 25th, it deepened to 972 millibars and was located along the Rhode Island and Massachusetts border. From there, a rare occurrence happened as the low pressure system retrograded or move westward. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the 972 millibar low was in Connecticut; at 7 a.m. EST it weakened to 979 millibars and was over New York City. It spent the rest of the daylight hours weakening over the New York City area and then drifted slowly north that night. The heaviest snow across a widespread area occurred during the retrogression process, but the intensity of the snow decreased once the low pressure system weakened.

A complex area of low pressure from the Ohio Valley to the Carolinas tracked east and intensified into a major winter storm off the Delamarva Peninsula from Tuesday the 9th to early Wednesday the 10th. The storm then tracked slowly northeast to a position well off the New Jersey Coast by early on Thursday the 11th. Snow spread into northeast Pennsylvania from the evening of the 9th to the early morning of the 10th. The snow became heavy at times across northeast Pennsylvania during the day Wednesday before tapering off by Wednesday evening. Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Strong winds behind the storm caused considerable blowing and drifting snow through Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county ranged from 6 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure organized off the southeast U.S. coast from Wednesday night, the 24th, to early Thursday the 25th. The low moved north along the eastern seaboard and became a major winter storm as it reached the tip of Long Island Thursday evening. The storm then track westward into the lower Hudson Valley and into New Jersey by Friday as it weakened. Light snow and flurries spread north into northeast Pennsylvania during the early morning hours Thursday the 25th. The snow became heavy Thursday afternoon and night. In addition to the heavy snows, north winds increased to 20 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph, leading to near blizzard conditions and considerable blowing and drifting snow. By the time the snow tapered to flurries, Friday morning the 26th, storm total snowfall ranged from 10 to 20 inches in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county, with amounts ranging from 10 inches in Wilmot to 16 inches in Windham Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dropped 20 to 30 inches of snow from Philadelphia south in Eastern Pennsylvania, 10 to 20 inches across the northern Philadelphia suburbs, 6 to 12 inches across Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and less than 6 inches in the Poconos from  the late afternoon of the 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. The 28.5 inches of snow that fell in Philadelphia became the second highest single snowfall event on history, only surpassed by the 30.7 inches of snow that fell during the Blizzard of January 1996.||Snow spread from south to north across Eastern Pennsylvania from late in the afternoon on the 5th into the middle of the evening of the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times during the first half of the day on the 6th across southeast Pennsylvania and Berks County. The snow ended during the morning of the 6th in the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos and during the afternoon of the 6th in southeast Pennsylvania. ||Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell declared a statewide disaster emergency. Many county and municipalities declared their own snow emergencies. National Guard troops were deployed to assist the State Police. Many roads and highways in southeastern Pennsylvania were impassable. Speed limits were reduced on the Delaware River bridges. Amtrak canceled train service. The SEPTA regional rail and bus lines suspended service on the 6th in and around Philadelphia. Airlines canceled flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport on the 6th. They resumed on the 7th. Many businesses, stores and malls were closed on the 6th. Events were canceled or postponed. Philadelphia schools were closed on Monday the 8th. Where the snow was relatively lighter, numerous accidents and fender benders were reported in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. In Berks County, several suspected heart attacks from shoveling snow occurred. A head-on collision in Cumru Township caused an injury. In West Lawn, a pedestrian was injured after he was struck by a vehicle and tossed from Westbound U.S. Route 422 onto Penn Avenue.  The snow was relatively fluffy and caused very few power outages. PECO Energy reported only 200 homes and businesses lost power in Bucks and Montgomery Counties.||Representative snowfall included 29.5 inches in Folcroft and Newtown Square (Delaware County), 28.5 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 25.5 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 23.0 inches in New London (Chester County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 22.0 inches in the Roxborough (Philadelphia County), 20.0 inches in Norristown (Montgomery County), 18.3 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 15.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 11.0 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 10.5 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 7.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 4.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 1.0 inch in Pocono Summit (Monroe County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east across northern Mexico and emerged in the Gulf of Mexico near Brownsville, Texas on the morning of the 4th. The low pressure system moved east and was located near New Orleans, Louisiana at 7 p.m. EST on the 4th and Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. The low pressure system then started to move northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th; Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 1 a.m. EST on the 6th; the Virginia coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 6th and then drifted slowly east off the lower Delmarva Peninsula the rest of the day on the 6th. The storm track was perfect for heavy snow in southeastern Pennsylvania, but became less favorable (too far southeast) for places farther to the north.

The third widespread major winter storm of the month of February affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the early morning of the 25th into the middle of the day on the 26th. In the Poconos, snowfall averaged 12 to 24 inches. In the Lehigh Valley, snowfall averaged around one foot and in southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia 6 to 12 inches. The lightest snowfall occurred in Berks County. Snow began falling before dawn on the 25th. In and around Philadelphia, precipitation initially started as rain before changing to snow by 7 a.m. EST. Except for the Poconos and parts of the Lehigh Valley, the snow that fell during the day on the 25th had a hard time accumulating on roadways that mainly stayed wet. Once the sun set, both the snowfall intensity and wind increased. But unlike the previous winter storm, the wind increased first and then the snow became heavier. Thus less power outages occurred. The snow fell heavy at times overnight on the 25th and decreased in intensity during the day on the 26th. The snow ended around Noon EST, except lingered into the afternoon in the Poconos. The strongest winds occurred from 6 p.m. EST on the 25th and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th. ||Schools were closed on the 25th in Philadelphia. Most schools in Eastern Pennsylvania were closed on the 26th. Municipal meetings were canceled as were sports games. Hundreds of flights were canceled going in and out of Philadelphia International Airport. Snow drifts forced the closure of Pennsylvania State Route 10 in Chester County. Because the winds increased before the heavier snow, PECO Energy reported less than 1,000 of its customers lost power in Southeastern Pennsylvania. A complex area of low pressure from the Ohio Valley to the Carolinas tracked east and intensified into a major winter storm off the Delamarva Peninsula from Tuesday the 9th to early Wednesday the 10th. The storm then tracked slowly northeast to a position well off the New Jersey Coast by early on Thursday the 11th. Snow spread into northeast Pennsylvania from the evening of the 9th to the early morning of the 10th. The snow became heavy at times across northeast Pennsylvania during the day Wednesday before tapering off by Wednesday evening. Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Strong winds behind the storm caused considerable blowing and drifting snow through Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the northern parts of the county ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure organized off the southeast U.S. coast from Wednesday night, the 24th, to early Thursday the 25th. The low moved north along the eastern seaboard and became a major winter storm as it reached the tip of Long Island Thursday evening. The storm then track westward into the lower Hudson Valley and into New Jersey by Friday as it weakened. Light snow and flurries spread north into northeast Pennsylvania during the early morning hours Thursday the 25th. The snow became heavy Thursday afternoon and night. In addition to the heavy snows, north winds increased to 20 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph, leading to near blizzard conditions and considerable blowing and drifting snow. By the time the snow tapered to flurries, Friday morning the 26th, storm total snowfall ranged from 10 to 20 inches in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county, with amounts ranging from around 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed off the North Carolina coast and tracked slowly northeast to off the Delmarva Peninsula by Wednesday morning the 24th. The storm then tracked out to sea. Light snow, mixed with rain, spread into northeast Pennsylvania, mainly east of Interstate 81, during the daylight hours of Tuesday the 23rd. The snow became heavier and steadier Tuesday night and tapered off Wednesday morning the 24th. The heaviest snow fell in Pike county, PA where between 5 and 17 inches fell. The heaviest snow was found over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 17 inches, with the highest amounts reported from the higher elevations in the central part of the county. Lower amounts of 3 to 5 inches occurred in the southern part of the county along the Delaware River valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

A series of low pressure systems that brought between two and four inches of rain from the evening of the 28th into the early morning of the 31st. Coupled with already wet antecedent conditions, this caused renewed flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania. The heaviest rain came in two shots, one that fell from the evening of the 28th into the early morning of the 29th and the second that fell during the daylight hours on the 30th.||The heavy rain was caused by a series of low pressure systems. The first one emerged from the southern Rockies on the morning of the 27th, reached the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 28th and started to weaken. Another low pressure system formed on its occluded front farther to the south and by 2 a.m. EDT on the 29th reached the Washington D.C. Metropolitan Area. This low pressure system too weakened as yet another one formed along the Carolina Coast during the morning of the 29th. This low pressure system moved slowly north and at 8 p.m. EDT on the 29th was located off of the North Carolina coast. It continued to intensify and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 30th, it was a 992 millibar low pressure system east of Atlantic City, New Jersey and at 2 p.m. EDT on the 30th, a 988 millibar low pressure system just south of Long Island, New York. From there it was captured by the upper level low and slowly weakened. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 30th, the surface low pressure system was located over central Long Island and then drifted very slowly southeast overnight.||This culminated a much wetter than normal month of March for Southeastern Pennsylvania. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the monthly precipitation total of 7.33 inches was the third wettest March on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 356 p.m. EDT through 910 p.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 8.77 feet at 630 p.m. EDT. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 339 p.m. EDT through 628 p.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 9.35 feet at 445 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 3.30 inches in Rockledge, 3.13 inches in Ardmore, 3.05 inches in Wynnewood and 2.81 inches in Willow Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four days of rain, heaviest on the 13th, culminated in flooding across southeastern Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals ranged from around 2 inches in the Poconos to 3 to 5 inches in the local Philadelphia area. Periods of rain started during the morning of the 12th and on the 13th fell at the heaviest during the daylight hours. Periods of lighter rain persisted into the 14th and 15th. The area avoided the heaviest rain that fell with the system over New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a slow moving low pressure system that had a tremendous fetch of moist air from the Atlantic Ocean. The low pressure system moved from the South Carolina coast on the morning of the 12th, into Virginia on the morning of the 13th, to Delaware on the morning of the 14th and then drifted slowly east over the next two days as it was captured by an upper level system. ||The flooding occurred on the weekend when eastern daylight time began. Some of the river flooding started on Eastern Standard Time and ended on Eastern Daylight Time, we apologize for any confusion this causes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 3.11 inches in Bath, 2.54 inches in in Easton and 2.22 inches in Northampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front triggered strong to severe thunderstorms that produced dime to golf ball size hail across central Pennsylvania during the mid-afternoon. The storms were slow-moving and trained over the ridges of the south-central mountains, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding in the hilly terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the heavy rain and strong winds downed weak trees, tree limbs and wires throughout Eastern Pennsylvania and affected Bucks County the hardest. The strongest wind gusts mainly occurred during the afternoon of the 13th. Over 200,000 homes and businesses lost power.  Several homes were damaged by fallen trees, one roof was damaged and one motorist was injured when struck by a downed tree. Several roads were closed because of downed trees. A series of low pressure systems that brought between two and four inches of rain from the evening of the 28th into the early morning of the 31st. Coupled with already wet antecedent conditions, this caused renewed flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania. The heaviest rain came in two shots, one that fell from the evening of the 28th into the early morning of the 29th and the second that fell during the daylight hours on the 30th.||The heavy rain was caused by a series of low pressure systems. The first one emerged from the southern Rockies on the morning of the 27th, reached the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 28th and started to weaken. Another low pressure system formed on its occluded front farther to the south and by 2 a.m. EDT on the 29th reached the Washington D.C. Metropolitan Area. This low pressure system too weakened as yet another one formed along the Carolina Coast during the morning of the 29th. This low pressure system moved slowly north and at 8 p.m. EDT on the 29th was located off of the North Carolina coast. It continued to intensify and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 30th, it was a 992 millibar low pressure system east of Atlantic City, New Jersey and at 2 p.m. EDT on the 30th, a 988 millibar low pressure system just south of Long Island, New York. From there it was captured by the upper level low and slowly weakened. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 30th, the surface low pressure system was located over central Long Island and then drifted very slowly southeast overnight.||This culminated a much wetter than normal month of March for Southeastern Pennsylvania. At the Philadelphia International Airport, the monthly precipitation total of 7.33 inches was the third wettest March on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 410 a.m. EDT through 842 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 7.62 feet at 7 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 3.03 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 2.30 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

A late night cold frontal passage triggered strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Aston Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger a couple of strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in and around Easton in eastern Northampton County. Wind gusts were measured at 65 mph in Williams Township and 58 mph in Forks Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger a couple of strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in Northampton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating of a very moist low level air mass aided severe thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening over the south central mountains and lower Susquehanna valley. Despite weak upper level support and vertical wind shear, several storms produced damaging winds and hail up to one inch in diameter. In addition, this environment proved to be favorable for slow-moving/training storms which resulted in localized flash flooding in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains in excess of 3 inches in a few hours caused flash flooding in west-central Lancaster County during the evening. The areas most affected included Columbia and Marietta Boroughs and much of West Hempfield Township. Several municipal roads including State Routes 23 and 441 were closed due to flooding. A mudslide on SR 411 contributed to a three-vehicle accident. A several roads were closed in the Willow Street/Pequea Township and Lancaster areas. The Rheems Fire Department reported areas of flash flooding in Maytown. Route 30 was closed near Columbia. Several basements were flooded. ||The flash flooding eventually transitioned to areal flooding later in the evening before receding around midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage helped trigger widely scattered showers and strong thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 3rd across southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a rain shower knocked over an oak tree on West Cherry Lane in Franconia Township and caused minor damage to a house.

In addition, this environment proved to be favorable for slow-moving/training storms which resulted in localized flash flooding in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph downed several large tree limbs on the west side of Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger clusters of showers and thunderstorms across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs during the late evening on the 2nd into the early morning of the 3rd. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches. Runoff from the heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding along a couple of the creeks later in the day on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 408 a.m. EDT through 533 a.m. EDT on the 3rd. It crested at 4.14 feet at 5 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.51 inches in Pottstown.
