EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four days of rain, heaviest on the 13th, culminated in flooding across southeastern Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals ranged from around 2 inches in the Poconos to 3 to 5 inches in the local Philadelphia area. Periods of rain started during the morning of the 12th and on the 13th fell at the heaviest during the daylight hours. Periods of lighter rain persisted into the 14th and 15th. The area avoided the heaviest rain that fell with the system over New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a slow moving low pressure system that had a tremendous fetch of moist air from the Atlantic Ocean. The low pressure system moved from the South Carolina coast on the morning of the 12th, into Virginia on the morning of the 13th, to Delaware on the morning of the 14th and then drifted slowly east over the next two days as it was captured by an upper level system. ||The flooding occurred on the weekend when eastern daylight time began. Some of the river flooding started on Eastern Standard Time and ended on Eastern Daylight Time, we apologize for any confusion this causes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Honey Brook was above its 7 foot flood stage from 617 p.m. EST through 1046 p.m. EST on the 13th. It crested at 7.57 feet at 815 p.m. EDT on the 13th. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Dowingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 419 p.m. EST on the 13th through 1235 a.m. EST on the 14th. It crested at 9.64 feet at 845 p.m. EST on the 13th. The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 920 p.m. EST on the 13th through 1105 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 530 a.m. EDT on the 14th. The French Creek at Phoenixville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 340 p.m. EST on the 13th through 752 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 9.35 feet at 645 p.m. EST on the 13th.  The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 321 p.m. EST through 913 p.m. EST on the 13th. It crested at 8.18 feet at 6 p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals were 3.47 inches in West Chester, 3.36 inches in Unionville. 3.14 inches in Honey Brook and 2.50 inches in Kenneth Square.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An up the Delaware Bay southerly flow that combined with fresh water runoff and astronomical spring tides associated with the full moon caused moderate tidal flooding with the overnight high tide on the 28th. Minor tidal flooding also occurred with the overnight high tides on the 27th and 29th.||The highest tide in Philadelphia reached 9.21 feet above mean lower low water.  Moderate tidal flooding begins at 9.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The tidal flooding was assisted by a series of low pressure systems. The first one emerged from the southern Rockies on the morning of the 27th, reached the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 28th and started to weaken. Another low pressure system formed on its occluded front farther to the south and by 2 a.m. EDT on the 29th reached the Washington D.C. Metropolitan Area. This helped produce the up the Delaware Bay southeast flow.  This low pressure system too weakened as yet another one formed along the Carolina Coast during the morning of the 29th. This low pressure system moved slowly north and at 8 p.m. EDT on the 29th was located off of the North Carolina coast. It continued to intensify and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 30th, it was east of Atlantic City, New Jersey and at 2 p.m. EDT on the 30th, just south of Long Island, New York. From there it was captured by the upper level low and slowly weakened and drifted southeast.

The combination of an increase in precipitation intensity and the passage of an upper level low pressure system caused the rain to change to snow over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the afternoon of the 30th. The snow fell at a heavy enough rate to accumulate even on roadways and caused numerous accidents. In Carbon County, U.S. Route 209 was closed between Lansford and Nesquehoning into the evening after a vehicle slid off the roadway and slid down its side. In Nesquehoning, a Jeep burst into flames after it skidded and ended on its side off Pennsylvania State Route 93. The driver was rescued. Other accidents were reported along the same roadway. In Monroe County, about twenty weather related accidents were reported. Accidents were concentrated along Long Pond Road and Pennsylvania State Route 196. Accidents were also reported on Pennsylvania State Route 423 (a rolled over Sports Utility Vehicle with an injury) and Interstates 80 and 380.  About one to four inches of snow accumulated during the afternoon. By the evening of the 30th, the precipitation either ended or the intensity became light enough for the precipitation to change back to rain.||The wintry weather was caused by the combination of a low pressure system that moved east of New Jersey during the day on the 30th and its associated upper level system that passed over Pennsylvania. The surface low pressure system moved slowly north from off of the North Carolina coast on the evening of the 29th and reached Long Island, New York during the evening of the 30th.

A backdoor cold front triggered strong to severe thunderstorms that produced dime to golf ball size hail across central Pennsylvania during the mid-afternoon. The storms were slow-moving and trained over the ridges of the south-central mountains, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding in the hilly terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding closed Knob Run Road at the intersection of Johnstown Road along Route 164. Flood waters also covered portions of Ski Gap/Right Hand Gap Road and the intersection of Johnstown/Polecat Road. The roads are located over hilly terrain in rural areas of Greenfield and Freedom Townships. Local residents reported a rapid rise along Poplar Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were scattered across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and southwest Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front. Significant damaging winds associated with a large bow echo occured along a path from near Steubenville, Ohio to Latrobe, Pennsylvania. From this bow echo the highest recorded wind gusts at ASOS observation sites were 71 MPH at Wheeling, WV, 66 MPH at Allegheny County Airport in West Mifflin, PA, and 86 MPH at Latrobe, PA. At least 150,000 homes lost power at some point during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS at Arnold Palmer Regional airport in Latrobe reported a wind gust of 86 MPH. The wind pushed a Piper Cherokee secured by wheel chocks into a fence. Minor damge was also reported to buildings at the terminal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd.  A cold front trailing the low moved east across the area causing showers and thunderstorms to develop.  A few of the stronger thunderstorms became severe.  One of the thunderstorms also spawned a tornado in Crawford County.  An EF0 tornado did minimal damage near Conneautville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in Crawford County just to the northwest of Conneautville.  The initial touchdown was just to the west of North Townline Road about a half mile north of State Route 198.  The tornado continued northeast on the ground for over a mile before lifting after crossing Fisher Road.  The damage path was no more than a 50 yards in width and consisted of around 200 downed trees.  A barn along the damage path also sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed near a warm frontal zone over the lower Susquehanna valley during the late afternoon and early evening. The storms produced significant downburst wind damage with peak wind speeds between 60 and 90 mph, knocking down multiple large trees and branches. Considerable structural damage also resulted from the severe winds. Isolated large hail was reported up to one-inch in diameter. A brief EF0 tornado, embedded within a larger swath of straight-line wind damage, touched down in the Thousand Trails campground with peak winds around 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team from the National Weather Service in State College confirmed an EF0 tornado at the Thousand Trails Campground on 493 S. Mt. Pleasant Road in South Annville Township. The touch-down was very brief with only a 200 yard path length and 50 yard path width. The maximum winds were estimated around 80 mph. The main damage was concentrated within a wooded area near the back of the campground facility. Several trailers sustained damage from falling trees and branches. The tornado was embedded within a broader area of thunderstorm straight-line wind damage that extended approximately seven miles from just east of Hershey into southwest Lebanon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lingered over the Upper Ohio Valley on June 23rd and 24th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front during the afternoon.  Several of the thunderstorms became severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic Coast early on February 25th.  This low deepened considerably and moved slowly up the East Coast on the 25th and 26th.  A trough stretching northwest from this low lingered over the Upper Ohio Valley from February 25th into the 27th.  Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie in association with the trough caused lake effect snow showers to develop and intensify on the 25th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then affected portions of northwestern Pennsylvania through the afternoon hours of the 26th.  The snow finally diminished during the evening hours of 26th.  Snowfall totals over portions of Northwest Pennsylvania exceeded 15 inches on the 25th and 26th.  Northwest winds gusted to more than 25 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 30 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.   Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  Some light freezing rain and drizzle also mixed with the snow at times during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  A slight coating of ice was reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed in Erie County during the morning hours of February 25th and quickly intensified.  A period of moderate to heavy snow occurred from late morning into the early afternoon hours.  The snow showers then diminished with mainly light snow reported during the second half of the afternoon and most of the evening.  The snow showers began to increase in intensity again just after midnight on the 26th with periods of moderate to heavy snow then occurring into the afternoon.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Areas of light freezing rain and drizzle mixed with the snow showers during the afternoon of the 26th.  A slight glazing of ice was reported at a few locations.  More light snow fell late in the afternoon and during the evening of the 26th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the evening.  In addition, northwest winds gusted to more than 25 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 30 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.  Snow totals across inland Erie County during this event ranged from 8 to 10 inches just south of Interstate 90 to more 20 inches at a few locations.  Snowfall totals from the 25th and 26th received from snowfall spotters included: 25 inches at Franklin Center; 24.0 inches at Pont; 22.0 inches southwest of North East; 19.0 inches at Edinboro and 14.3 inches in Amity Township.  Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered over Tennessee at daybreak on February 9th moved rapidly northeast during the day.  By early evening, the low was centered over Central Ohio.  The low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania just after midnight on the 10th before shifting to the east coast later in the day.  Snow associated with this low spread into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours of the 9th.  Mainly light snow fell during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th.  Winds eventually became westerly off of Lake Erie early on the 10th which helped to enhance the snowfall. The snow intensity picked up greatly during the early morning hours of the 10th with periods of moderate to heavy snow then reported into the afternoon of the 10th.  The snow eventually tapered off during the early evening hours of the 10th.  Snowfall totals on the 9th and 10th exceeded 10 inches over portions of Erie and Crawford County.  In addition, winds gusted to as much as 25 mph from the late evening hours of the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th.  The winds and snow combined to create considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this storm and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Erie County during the early afternoon hours of February 9th.  Mainly light snow was reported during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th.  Winds eventually became westerly off of Lake Erie around midnight on the 10th which helped to enhance the snowfall.  The snow intensity picked up greatly during the early morning hours of the 10th with periods of moderate to heavy snow then reported into the early afternoon hours of the 10th.  The snow eventually tapered off around sunset on the 10th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour with visibilities less than a half mile.  Snowfall totals on the 9th and 10th ranged from 8 to 12 inches over inland Erie County with most of the accumulation occurring on the 10th.  Storm total reports received from snow spotters included:  11.6 inches at Franklin Center; 10.2 inches at Corry and 9.5 inches at Colt Station.  In addition, winds gusted to as much as 25 mph from the late evening hours of the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th.  The winds and snow combined to create considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this storm and several accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced significant wind damage and isolated one-inch diameter hail across portions of the lower Susquehanna Valley during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported funnel/rotating wall cloud dissipated before touching down north of Chambersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed near a warm frontal zone over the lower Susquehanna valley during the late afternoon and early evening. The storms produced significant downburst wind damage with peak wind speeds between 60 and 90 mph, knocking down multiple large trees and branches. Considerable structural damage also resulted from the severe winds. Isolated large hail was reported up to one-inch in diameter. A brief EF0 tornado, embedded within a larger swath of straight-line wind damage, touched down in the Thousand Trails campground with peak winds around 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant thunderstorm winds estimated between 60-80 mph damaged eight buildings at the Milton Hershey School. Numerous large trees and branches were down, some causing damage to buildings and roofs. A farm house across from the school lost its roof and two other nearby barns and outbuildings sustained significant damage. Nearly 60 acres of fruit, corn and soybean crops were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon into the first half of the evening on the 8th. Strong winds persisted longer overnight over the higher terrain of the Poconos. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and tore down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. About 31,000 homes and businesses lost power. In southeastern Pennsylvania, about 25,000 PECO Energy homes and businesses lost power. In Pottstown (Montgomery County), a male driver was injured on South Street after a tree limb cracked his wind shield. In Bucks County, the majority of the Pennsylvania Power and Light outages were concentrated in East Rockhill Township. In the Lehigh Valley, in Upper Milford Township (Lehigh County), a minivan and pick-up truck were damaged by downed trees. Another tree fell against a home in Bethlehem. Outages in Lehigh County were concentrated in Upper Saucon Township.||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 55 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 51 mph at Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 50 mph in Kenneth Square (Chester County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 47 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County) and 46 mph at the Willow Grove NAS in Montgomery County.||The strong west winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system moving toward the east from the Northern Plains.
A cold front approached the area from the northeast, with showers and thunderstorms developing during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three 40 foot pine trees were blown down. One inch hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic Coast early on February 25th.  This low deepened considerably and moved slowly up the East Coast on the 25th and 26th.  A trough stretching northwest from this low lingered over the Upper Ohio Valley from February 25th into the 27th.  Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie in association with the trough caused lake effect snow showers to develop and intensify on the 25th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then affected portions of northwestern Pennsylvania through the afternoon hours of the 26th.  The snow finally diminished during the evening hours of 26th.  Snowfall totals over portions of Northwest Pennsylvania exceeded 15 inches on the 25th and 26th.  Northwest winds gusted to more than 25 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 30 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.   Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  Some light freezing rain and drizzle also mixed with the snow at times during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  A slight coating of ice was reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed in Crawford County during the morning hours of February 25th and quickly intensified.  A period of moderate to heavy snow occurred from late morning into the early afternoon hours.  The snow showers then diminished with mainly light snow reported during the second half of the afternoon and most of the evening.  The snow showers began to increase in intensity again just after midnight on the 26th with periods of moderate to heavy snow then occurring into early afternoon.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Lighter snow fell late in the afternoon and during the evening of the 26th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the evening.  In addition, northwest winds gusted to more than 25 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 30 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create  near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.  Snow totals across Crawford County during this event ranged from 6 inches in the southeast corner of the county to more than a foot along the northern border.   Snowfall totals from the 25th and 26th received from snowfall spotters included: 15.5 inches near Crossingville; 14.0 inches at Conneautville; 12.6 inches at Linesville and 10.0 inches at Meadville.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms crossed the Commonwealth during the mid afternoon and early evening hours, producing multiple reports of straight line damaging winds. The primary damage area started in Clearfield County along Interstate 80 and extended east-southeast through the south central mountains and into the lower Susquehanna Valley. Thunderstorm structures organized into bowing line segments, which tapped and accentuated anomalously strong environmental winds in the middle to lower atmosphere. Numerous measured peak wind gusts between 50 and 80 mph were reported via ASOS. One injury occurred in Cumberland County (Monroe Township) when a tree fell onto a vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph collapsed a barn in New Enterprise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with a very moist air mass over central Pennsylvania to produce isolated damaging thunderstorm winds and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph toppled several trees on Creek Road just north of Route 928.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with a very moist air mass over central Pennsylvania to produce isolated damaging thunderstorm winds and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding over portions of the city of Harrisburg. Several roads were flooded and closed, including Cameron Street, Berryhill Road, Arsenal Boulevard, Locust Lane and Elmerton Avenue. Four water rescues were performed, when vehicles became stranded in flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced multiple reports of wind damage during the late afternoon and evening hours across central Pennsylvania. Thunderstorm winds gusted as high as 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 80 mph uplifted a large (26x40) garage roof and carried it approximately 200 feet from the original structure. The damage occurred in the area of 169 Queen Street in Reedsville (Brown Township). Additional debris was lifted nearly 75 feet over surrounding obstructions. Several homes in the immediate area were affected by downed wires. The damage was confined to the garage roof with only minor residual damage noted. No injuries were reported.

The primary damage area started in Clearfield County along Interstate 80 and extended east-southeast through the south central mountains and into the lower Susquehanna Valley. Thunderstorm structures organized into bowing line segments, which tapped and accentuated anomalously strong environmental winds in the middle to lower atmosphere. Numerous measured peak wind gusts between 50 and 80 mph were reported via ASOS. One injury occurred in Cumberland County (Monroe Township) when a tree fell onto a vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph toppled numerous trees in and around the city of Johnstown. Several thousand customers experienced power outages which lasted for approximately 24 to 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an increase in precipitation intensity and the passage of an upper level low pressure system caused the rain to change to snow over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the afternoon of the 30th. The snow fell at a heavy enough rate to accumulate even on roadways and caused numerous accidents. In Carbon County, U.S. Route 209 was closed between Lansford and Nesquehoning into the evening after a vehicle slid off the roadway and slid down its side. In Nesquehoning, a Jeep burst into flames after it skidded and ended on its side off Pennsylvania State Route 93. The driver was rescued. Other accidents were reported along the same roadway. In Monroe County, about twenty weather related accidents were reported. Accidents were concentrated along Long Pond Road and Pennsylvania State Route 196. Accidents were also reported on Pennsylvania State Route 423 (a rolled over Sports Utility Vehicle with an injury) and Interstates 80 and 380.  About one to four inches of snow accumulated during the afternoon. By the evening of the 30th, the precipitation either ended or the intensity became light enough for the precipitation to change back to rain.||The wintry weather was caused by the combination of a low pressure system that moved east of New Jersey during the day on the 30th and its associated upper level system that passed over Pennsylvania. The surface low pressure system moved slowly north from off of the North Carolina coast on the evening of the 29th and reached Long Island, New York during the evening of the 30th.

A retreating warm frontal zone and associated sharp temperature gradient combined with steep mid-level lapse rates to produce isolated large hail.

A cold front triggered multiple severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds over the south-central mountains and lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon and evening hours. The largest hailstones were estimated at two inches in diameter. The large hail caused significant damage and crop loss over the lower Susquehanna Valley, but the total acreage and dollar amount of the losses are unknown. The USDA issued a Disaster Declaration for the crop loss resulting from the hail in York, Adams, Cumberland, Dauphin and Lancaster counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of deepening low pressure moved east across northern Ohio during the evening hours of May 7th.  This low entered northwestern Pennsylvania around midnight on the 8th and by midday had reached northern New York state.  Showers and thunderstorms associated with the low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania  during the evening hours of the 7th.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe and moved across portions of Erie and Crawford Counties.  There were reports of damaging winds in both counties.  A strong cold front trailing the low moved across western Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in Crawford County during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees throughout Crawford County.  A few power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage overnight on the 16th helped trigger strong to locally severe thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across southwestern Berks County in Womelsdorf, South Heidelberg and Spring Townships. The worst damage occurred in the West Wyomissing section of Spring Township where approximately 20 homes were damaged by either the wind directly (mainly shingles torn off) or downed trees. A downed tree broke through the roof of a home on Hillvale Avenue and displaced the family. The damage was concentrated in a two to three block area from Fritztown Road to Lincoln Avenue. A downed tree damaged two vehicles on Lincoln Avenue. The siding and shingles of another home were torn away. A trampoline was tossed into a vehicle. About 400 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County, most had the power restored by 10 p.m. EDT that evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team from the National Weather Service investigated thunderstorm wind damage that began near the intersection of Tadpole and Marengo Roads and continued east for about 3.5 miles. The damage varied in width from about 1 mile wide at the start of the damage swath and narrowed to about 200 yards. 70 to 100 trees were blown down. Several barns and out-buildings sustained roof damage and four homes were damaged by falling trees. The homes also sustained siding and shingle damage. The most significant and concentrated tree damage occurred along an just east of Pine Hollow Road. Based on the damage to the trees and structures, the National Weather Service confirmed a macroburst with estimated maximum winds between 80 and 90 mph.

Strong heating of a very moist low level air mass aided severe thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening over the south central mountains and lower Susquehanna valley. Despite weak upper level support and vertical wind shear, several storms produced damaging winds and hail up to one inch in diameter. In addition, this environment proved to be favorable for slow-moving/training storms which resulted in localized flash flooding in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains in excess of 3 inches in a few hours caused flash flooding in west-central Lancaster County during the evening. The areas most affected included Columbia and Marietta Boroughs and much of West Hempfield Township. Several municipal roads including State Routes 23 and 441 were closed due to flooding. A mudslide on SR 411 contributed to a three-vehicle accident. A several roads were closed in the Willow Street/Pequea Township and Lancaster areas. The Rheems Fire Department reported areas of flash flooding in Maytown. Route 30 was closed near Columbia. Several basements were flooded. ||The flash flooding eventually transitioned to areal flooding later in the evening before receding around midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Appalachian lee side trough and an approaching cold front both helped produce severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 14th. One particularly strong supercell thunderstorm dropped hail as large as tennis balls in Lehigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter to golf ball size hail from Schnecksville in North Whitehall Township into Allentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of heavy thunderstorm rains produced isolated flash flooding across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rain from slow-moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding in west-central Franklin County. Numerous roads were closed in Peters, Hamilton and Letterkenny Townships. Several cars and recreational vehicles were submerged in flood waters of 5 feet or more at the Twin Bridge Meadow Family campground near Chambersburg, where at least 10-15 campers were rescued after being stranded. A wall collapse occurred at a residence in St. Thomas Township due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered multiple severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds over the south-central mountains and lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon and evening hours. The largest hailstones were estimated at two inches in diameter. The large hail caused significant damage and crop loss over the lower Susquehanna Valley, but the total acreage and dollar amount of the losses are unknown. The USDA issued a Disaster Declaration for the crop loss resulting from the hail in York, Adams, Cumberland, Dauphin and Lancaster counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Juniata and Oliver Townships near Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered severe thunderstorms across north-central Pennsylvania from the mid-afternoon into the evening hours. The severe storms produced several reports of thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS meteorologists surveyed significant thunderstorm wind damage in the Centre Hall area. Thunderstorm winds estimated between 60 and 70 mph knocked down numerous trees and wires near the junction of Route 144 and Sinking Creek Road. Several utility poles were knocked down along Route 45 west of Old Fort. Sporadic tree damage was found in an area approximately two miles wide by four miles long, starting a few miles southwest of Centre Hall on Brush Valley Road. Minor roof damage to two structures was also observed. The downed utility poles and wires closed State Route 45 in both directions between Route 144 near Centre Hall and U.S. 322 at Oak Hall. The downed wires also resulted in over 500 power outages.

Strong heating of a very moist low level air mass aided severe thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening over the south central mountains and lower Susquehanna valley. Despite weak upper level support and vertical wind shear, several storms produced damaging winds and hail up to one inch in diameter. In addition, this environment proved to be favorable for slow-moving/training storms which resulted in localized flash flooding in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains in excess of 3 inches in a few hours caused flash flooding in west-central Lancaster County during the evening. The areas most affected included Columbia and Marietta Boroughs and much of West Hempfield Township. The flash flooding transitioned to areal flooding later that night before receding around midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two more days of unseasonably hot and humid weather in and around Philadelphia claimed the lives of two more people within the city. High temperatures reached in the mid 90s (95 and 96 degrees respectively) on the 27th and 28th and combined with the humid air mass to produce afternoon heat indices of around 100F.  The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging hotline fielded about 150 calls for assistance during the two days.  In Philadelphia, a 46-year-old woman was found dead in bed in a first floor bedroom of her home. All the doors and windows were shut and there was no air conditioning or fan.  An 88-year-old man was also found dead in bed on the first floor of his home. A box fan was running, but all the windows were closed. Thunderstorms and then a cold front that moved through the area ended the excessive heat.||The unseasonably hot weather throughout June made June 2010 the hottest June on record in Philadelphia. The monthly mean temperature of 78.2 degrees surpassed June of 1925 (78.0 degrees). The 15 days with maximum temperatures of 90 degrees or higher tied June 1943 for the most. It was also tied for the 9th warmest June on record (72.1 degrees) on record at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, tied for the 4th warmest June (74.6 degrees) on record at the Reading Regional Airport (Berks County) and the 7th warmest June on record (66.3 degrees) at the Pocono Regional Airport (Monroe County).

Severe thunderstorms caused considerable tree damage and two injuries during the afternoon of the 24th across southeastern Pennsylvania. PECO Energy reported about 215,000 customers lost power, the 11th worst outage in company history. Chester and Delaware Counties were hit the hardest. as of 1201 a.m. EDT on the 25th, 190,000 customers were without power. That number dropped to 152,000 at Noon EDT on the 25th and 75,000 at 11 p.m. EDT on the 25th. Power was not fully restored until the 28th. The utility had to replace 140 poles, 140 pole top transformers, 900 pole cross arms and twenty miles of electrical cable. Regional rail lines were affected as downed trees blocked some routes. SEPTA delayed or suspended train service south of Philadelphia. All train service was not fully restored until the evening of the 25th. Downed trees also blocked lanes of the Schuylkill Expressway in Philadelphia and the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Chester County. Philadelphia International's 75 mph wind gust was the third highest on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in South Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front remained stretched across the Upper Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of June 6th.  This front kept a very warm and humid airmass over  northwestern Pennsylvania.  A rapidly deepening area of low pressure moved quickly east across southern lower Michigan during the evening hours of June 5th.  This low eventually moved across Lake Erie during the early morning hours of the 6th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along the stationary front stretching east from the low.  These storms eventually moved into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of June 6th.  One of the stronger thunderstorms produced a downburst which overturned a camper trailer and killed a man.  The same thunderstorm later produced a tornado in western Erie County.  A second line of showers and storms affected Erie and Crawford Counties around daybreak on the 6th.  These storms developed ahead of a cold front trailing the low.  A tornado occurred in Crawford County just after daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather enveloped southeastern Pennsylvania on the 23rd and 24th. It culminated on the 24th with maximum temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and afternoon heat indices of around 100F. For many places this was already hotter than the hottest day in all of 2009. The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging helpline center referred thirty-three calls to a nurse and three to the city's 911 center. Maximum temperatures included 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 96 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 94 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County).  The hot and humid weather ended abruptly with the passage of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon on the 24th.

An area of low pressure located over the northern tip of the Lower Peninsula of Michigan and cold frontal system approaching from the west allowed for the development of a large convective complex to move through northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous large trees across Erie County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms caused considerable tree damage and two injuries during the afternoon of the 24th across southeastern Pennsylvania. PECO Energy reported about 215,000 customers lost power, the 11th worst outage in company history. Chester and Delaware Counties were hit the hardest. as of 1201 a.m. EDT on the 25th, 190,000 customers were without power. That number dropped to 152,000 at Noon EDT on the 25th and 75,000 at 11 p.m. EDT on the 25th. Power was not fully restored until the 28th. The utility had to replace 140 poles, 140 pole top transformers, 900 pole cross arms and twenty miles of electrical cable. Regional rail lines were affected as downed trees blocked some routes. SEPTA delayed or suspended train service south of Philadelphia. All train service was not fully restored until the evening of the 25th. Downed trees also blocked lanes of the Schuylkill Expressway in Philadelphia and the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Chester County. Philadelphia International's 75 mph wind gust was the third highest on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 65 knots or 75 mph at Philadelphia International Airport. This was the third highest gust ever recorded at the airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in the southern half of Philadelphia. There were 184 calls of downed trees with the Southwest Philadelphia and Cobbs Creek areas hit the hardest. A few streets were blocked by downed trees. Downed trees also blocked some of the lanes on the Schuylkill Expressway. Part of the roof was torn from the New Light Beulah Baptist Church on Bainbridge Street and crushed two vehicles.  Downed trees crushed a fence at Saint John Neumann Place in South Philadelphia. The awning of a banquet hall on West Oxford Street collapsed. The severe thunderstorm also lifted the tarp at Citizen Bank Baseball Park into the air. Trolley Wires were knocked down in the University City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lingered across the Upper Ohio Valley on June 4th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed to the north of this front and moved across the area.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe.  Many reports of large hail were received in Crawford and Erie County.  Some of the hail was as large as grapefruit.  Many vehicles were damage by the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of wind damage from downed trees, tree limbs to downed wires across the northeast half of Chester County. Many roadways were blocked and vehicles damaged by fallen trees. Trees also fell onto the Pennsylvania Turnpike. Hardest hit were Uwchlan, Wallace, West Vincent and Willistown Townships, but wind damage was also reported in North Coventry, Phoenixville, Charlestown, Downingtown, West Whiteland, West Goshen, East Goshen, West Chester, Westtown, Pocopson. Kenneth and Kenneth Square. Nearly 80,000 homes and businesses lost power and it took until June 28th for power to be fully restored. In West Chester, downed trees blocked the entrance to a hospital. Downed trees also damaged a home and garage and crushed a barn. In North Coventry, a downed tree punched a hole in the roof of a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same severe thunderstorm produced a swath of large hail (quarter to golf ball size) across eastern Delaware County from Haverford Township south through Yeadon Borough. The largest hail (golf ball size) was reported in Yeadon and ping pong ball size hail (1.5 inches in diameter) was reported in Drexel Hill in Upper Darby Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front remained stretched across the Upper Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of June 6th.  This front kept a very warm and humid airmass over  northwestern Pennsylvania.  A rapidly deepening area of low pressure moved quickly east across southern lower Michigan during the evening hours of June 5th.  This low eventually moved across Lake Erie during the early morning hours of the 6th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along the stationary front stretching east from the low.  These storms eventually moved into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of June 6th.  One of the stronger thunderstorms produced a downburst which overturned a camper trailer and killed a man.  The same thunderstorm later produced a tornado in western Erie County.  A second line of showers and storms affected Erie and Crawford Counties around daybreak on the 6th.  These storms developed ahead of a cold front trailing the low.  A tornado occurred in Crawford County just after daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado briefly touched down in extreme eastern Crawford County.  The touchdown occurred along Mageetown Road in rural Rome Township.  The tornado was on the ground for around a half mile before lifting.  Two homes along the damage path sustained roof damage.  A few trees were also downed.  The damage path appeared to be no wider than 20 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down near a camp ground on West Lake Road northeast of North Springfield.  From the site of the initial touchdown, the tornado continued on the ground intermittently for almost six miles.  The tornado eventually lifted for good east of Girard.  The tornado caused damage to a few buildings.  Most of the damage was minor but a church did sustain significant damage to one wall.  Several business on the east side of Girard sustained roof damage.  Significant tree damage occurred along portions of the damage path.  The same thunderstorm that produced this tornado was also responsible for the damaging downburst winds that killed a camper in western Erie County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large trees. A transformer fire occurred as a result of the downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northwestern Pennsylvania around midday on June 28th.  An isolated thunderstorm developed in Erie County and caused some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst caused a swath of damage in Mill Creek and Summit Townships.  Dozens of trees were downed in the area.   A hotel near the intersection of Interstate 90 and State Route 19 sustained roof damage.  A couple businesses nearby had windows blown out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst with wind gusts estimated to be at least 60 mph caused damage at a campground in western Erie County.  The campground is located along Lake Erie northwest of North Springfield.  Several trees were knocked down and a camper trailer overturned at the campground.  A 36 year old man was killed when the overturned camper landed on him.  A damage path containing dozens of downed trees continued east from the campground for at least a mile.  The same thunderstorm that produced this downburst later produced a weak tornado that caused damage from northeast of North Springfield to near Girard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure was located over Ontario, Canada on July 24th. A cold front stretched south from the low across the Great Lakes and into Texas. This front progressed east during the day. A strong line of convective showers and thunderstorms developed along the frontal boundary and affected the Upper Ohio Valley region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a couple of large trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike created a foot wide hole in the roof of a house on Jasmine Road  in Lower Southampton Township. It ignited a wire within the attic and started a fire. Six different municipal responders assisted with the fire which was contained within 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching and passing warm front triggered strong to severe thunderstorms during the evening of the 22nd across extreme southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst knocked down trees and fences, overturned tables and tossed umbrellas at Sesame Place. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered several broken lines of severe thunderstorms across south-central and southeastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon. A few discrete, organized storms developed over the lower Susquehanna Valley. The convective environment was characterized by strong surface heating of a moist low level air mass which resulted in moderate instability ahead of the approaching cold front. In addition, strong westerly wind flow and shear in the middle and upper atmosphere contributed to support organized storm structures that produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated large hail. The majority of the severe weather was confined to the lower Susquehanna Valley between 1300 and 1500 EDT.

A few discrete, organized storms developed over the lower Susquehanna Valley. The convective environment was characterized by strong surface heating of a moist low level air mass which resulted in moderate instability ahead of the approaching cold front. In addition, strong westerly wind flow and shear in the middle and upper atmosphere contributed to support organized storm structures that produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated large hail. The majority of the severe weather was confined to the lower Susquehanna Valley between 1300 and 1500 EDT.

Strong west winds affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon into the first half of the evening on the 8th. Strong winds persisted longer overnight over the higher terrain of the Poconos. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and tore down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. About 31,000 homes and businesses lost power. In southeastern Pennsylvania, about 25,000 PECO Energy homes and businesses lost power. In Pottstown (Montgomery County), a male driver was injured on South Street after a tree limb cracked his wind shield. In Bucks County, the majority of the Pennsylvania Power and Light outages were concentrated in East Rockhill Township. In the Lehigh Valley, in Upper Milford Township (Lehigh County), a minivan and pick-up truck were damaged by downed trees. Another tree fell against a home in Bethlehem. Outages in Lehigh County were concentrated in Upper Saucon Township.||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 55 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 51 mph at Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 50 mph in Kenneth Square (Chester County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 47 mph in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County) and 46 mph at the Willow Grove NAS in Montgomery County.||The strong west winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system moving toward the east from the Northern Plains.
A slowly westward moving backdoor cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in Rockland Township.

A very strong area of deepening low pressure moved east across northern Ohio during the evening hours of May 7th.  This low entered northwestern Pennsylvania around midnight on the 8th and by midday had reached northern New York state.  Showers and thunderstorms associated with the low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania  during the evening hours of the 7th.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe and moved across portions of Erie and Crawford Counties.  There were reports of damaging winds in both counties.  A strong cold front trailing the low moved across western Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in Crawford County during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds estimated to be as much as 65 mph downed trees throughout northern Erie County.   A home in Lawrence Park was damaged by a fallen tree.  Scattered power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across northeast Pennsylvania in the vicinity of a stalled frontal boundary. With a very moist air mass in place across the region, the storms that developed produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches occurred in a 2 hour time period over the Scranton area. This intense rainfall resulted in flash flooding. The west and south side of Scranton were the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Scranton area. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell in a two hour period of time, with the west and south-side of Scranton hardest hit. Numerous locations in Scranton were closed due to high water. Some cars had water up to the windows. Locations where cars were stranded due to high water included the intersection of Luzerne Street and Meridian Avenue, South Wyoming Avenue, Birney Avenue and Hickory Street. Large rocks and debris were reported on Molltke Avenue. A couple of homes had water up to the first floor level on Railroad Avenue. ||Flooding was not limited to the city of Scranton. A car was also swamped in the Borough of Taylor along Union Street. Saint John's Creek in Old Forge flooded a city park and a portion of Sibley Avenue where the occupants of a vehicle had to be rescued by boat. Rocks and debris were washed onto portions of Creek Road in Ransom Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather enveloped southeastern Pennsylvania on the 23rd and 24th. It culminated on the 24th with maximum temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and afternoon heat indices of around 100F. For many places this was already hotter than the hottest day in all of 2009. The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging helpline center referred thirty-three calls to a nurse and three to the city's 911 center. Maximum temperatures included 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 96 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 94 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County).  The hot and humid weather ended abruptly with the passage of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon on the 24th.

With a very moist airmass in place across the region, the storms that developed produced very heavy rainfall. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed across northern Wayne county due to high water. Over two feet of water was reported on a portion of the Dixie Highway in the Township of Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance pushed across the region during the afternoon. Showers and thunderstorms developed, and a few storms reached severe levels. Colder temperatures aloft caused many of the storms to produce hail, and strong winds were also reported.

Showers and thunderstorms developed over the area during the evening as a low pressure system moved through the region. One of the storms produced an EF1 tornado in northeast Bradford County, with damage to trees, power lines and a few homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Osbourne Hill Road in northeast Windham Township around 8:30 pm EDT. Numerous trees were uprooted and snapped as the tornado  tracked along Osbourne Hill Road, east toward Route 187. The heaviest damage was to a hillside close to where Osbourne Hill Road meets Route 187. Over 100 trees were snapped and uprooted on this hillside. The tornado then crossed Route 187 and uprooted and snapped trees along Huddle Road. One house had numerous trees down on the property, with one tree falling on the back of the house. Some minor roof damage was also done to this house. A camper was also flipped on Huddle Road. The tornado then tracked up a hillside east of Huddle Road and lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front remained stretched across the Upper Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of June 6th.  This front kept a very warm and humid airmass over  northwestern Pennsylvania.  A rapidly deepening area of low pressure moved quickly east across southern lower Michigan during the evening hours of June 5th.  This low eventually moved across Lake Erie during the early morning hours of the 6th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along the stationary front stretching east from the low.  These storms eventually moved into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of June 6th.  One of the stronger thunderstorms produced a downburst which overturned a camper trailer and killed a man.  The same thunderstorm later produced a tornado in western Erie County.  A second line of showers and storms affected Erie and Crawford Counties around daybreak on the 6th.  These storms developed ahead of a cold front trailing the low.  A tornado occurred in Crawford County just after daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of the summer season claimed two lives within Philadelphia. A 50-year-old male was found unresponsive outdoors on June 2nd at the intersection of 8th and Walnut Streets and pronounced dead later that day. A 77-year-old man was found dead in a second floor bedroom. All the windows were closed and there was no air conditioning or fans. High temperatures in Philadelphia were in the lower 90s from the 2nd through the 6th. The hottest day was the 6th with a high temperature of 93 degrees.

Increasing instability due to surface heating ahead of a cold front resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing across northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon. Strong winds in the lower and middle levels of the atmosphere caused several storms to produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered strong to severe thunderstorms from the Lehigh Valley southeast into the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area on the afternoon of the 25th. About 60,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest concentration in Lehigh County in the Lehigh Valley and Chester County in southeastern Pennsylvania. It took up to 24 hours for all power to be restored. One lightning related injury occurred in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in northeastern and eastern Bucks County. Trees were knocked down in both directions on Pennsylvania State Route 32 in Yardley Borough and in Lower Makefield Township. The roadway was blocked into the 26th in Lower Makefield Township. Two downed trees also crushed two vehicles. No injuries were reported. Both Bucks and Chester Counties took the brunt of the power outages (45,000) in PECO Energy's service area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and signs in Philadelphia. In the Torresdale section, a home was damaged by a downed tree. A large maple tree was blown down onto Roxborough Avenue between Houghton Street and Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms caused considerable tree damage and two injuries during the afternoon of the 24th across southeastern Pennsylvania. PECO Energy reported about 215,000 customers lost power, the 11th worst outage in company history. Chester and Delaware Counties were hit the hardest. as of 1201 a.m. EDT on the 25th, 190,000 customers were without power. That number dropped to 152,000 at Noon EDT on the 25th and 75,000 at 11 p.m. EDT on the 25th. Power was not fully restored until the 28th. The utility had to replace 140 poles, 140 pole top transformers, 900 pole cross arms and twenty miles of electrical cable. Regional rail lines were affected as downed trees blocked some routes. SEPTA delayed or suspended train service south of Philadelphia. All train service was not fully restored until the evening of the 25th. Downed trees also blocked lanes of the Schuylkill Expressway in Philadelphia and the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Chester County. Philadelphia International's 75 mph wind gust was the third highest on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced a swath of wind damage across the northeast half of Delaware County. Numerous trees and wires were knocked down. There were four times the normal number of calls into the county's 911 center. Because of the nearly 100,000 power outages in the county, dry ice was distributed in Havertown, Newtown Square and Springfield. A woman and her daughter were injured when a tree fell onto their vehicle in Lansdowne. A 300-year-old tree was knocked down onto Greenwood Avenue in the borough. Most of the borough lost power. In Upper Darby Township, the roof from the Today's Child Learning Center blew onto the SEPTA Regional Rail lines tracks near the Primos Station. The roof of another home was nearly torn away.  In all several homes and a park were damaged by fallen trees along Windermere Avenue. Another downed tree fell onto a moving vehicle, but the five occupants were not hurt. Firefighters rescued a 10-year-old boy with an iron lung. A Skywarn Spotter measured a wind gust of 76 mph. Because 15,000 residents lost power, an air conditioned comfort center was available at the Upper Darby High School Cafeteria.  In Haverford Township, a 30,000 volt transmission wire was knocked down and create a crater on a roadway. In Marple Township, windows were shattered and siding was stripped around the Lawrence Park Industrial Center. A large sign above the Five Guys Shopping Center was cracked. In Springfield Township around Dutton Circle and Valley View Road, windows and sidings were also damaged, roof shingles were also torn and at least one porch and vehicle were damaged by downed trees. The SEPTA Media/Elwyn line did not resume regional rail service until the evening of the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance pushed across the region during the afternoon. Showers and thunderstorms developed, and a few storms reached severe levels. Colder temperatures aloft caused many of the storms to produce hail, and strong winds were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An up to four day heat wave across southeastern Pennsylvania culminated with high temperatures in the mid 90s and afternoon heat indices of around 100F on the 10th. A pair of backdoor cold fronts ended the hot weather on the 12th. While there was a heat wave during the last couple of days in August (with some places equaling to recording the highest temperatures of the month then), the 10th was the last time during 2010 that heat indices reached that high. Actual highest temperatures for the 10th and 11th included 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Reading (Berks County) and 94 degrees in Doylestown.||August 2010 was the 8th warmest August on record in Philadelphia with an average temperature of 79.0 degrees. The summer of 2010 was the 7th warmest meteorological summer on record for the state of Pennsylvania since 1895 with an average temperature of 71.2 degrees. It was progressively warmer farther to the east in Philadelphia and was the warmest summer on record in Philadelphia with an average temperature of 79.6 degrees, the second warmest summer on record in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) at 68.9 degrees and Reading (Berks County) at 76.4 degrees and the ninth warmest summer in record in Allentown (Lehigh County) at 74.0 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed from near Williamsport, Pennsylvania south into Maryland in association with a surface trough and upper level disturbance. As the line lifted northeast into the northern tier of Pennsylvania, one storm became severe and produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts knocked down trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last heat wave in July culminated with some of the highest heat indices of the summer on the 24th and numerous high temperatures in the mid to upper 90s in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania. The combination of the heat and humidity produced heat index values of around 105 degrees on the 24th. The excessive heat caused four more heat related deaths in southeastern Pennsylvania, three in Philadelphia and one in Bucks County. In Bucks County, a 20-year-old severely autistic man died on the 24th after he was left in a parked van outside of a Langhorne care center. In Philadelphia, the heat claimed the life of a 66-year-old woman, a 90-year-old woman (found in an apartment with closed windows and a fan on) and a 56-year-old man. Delaware County opened cooling centers through the weekend of the 24th and 25th.||The heat wave ended with strong to severe thunderstorms that preceded a strong cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 25th. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Philadelphia, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), 95 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in London Grove (Chester County) and 93 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County).  The month of July was one of the hottest Julys on record in Eastern Pennsylvania. It was the 2nd hottest July on record in Philadelphia (monthly mean temperature of 81.7 degrees) and the 6th hottest July on record in Reading (monthly mean temperature of 78.8 degrees). It also tied July 1999 and 1995 for the most days with temperatures of 90 degrees or higher (20 days). Even the Poconos did not escape the heat as it was the third warmest July on record (71.8 degrees) in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

A warm front lifted north into northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon, putting the area into the warm and moist air mass of the warm sector, with temperatures in the mid 80s and dew points in the 70s. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of the front in this unstable air mass. Stronger storms developed across southeast Susquehanna county, with one storm in particular developing strong rotation as it moved southeast into Wayne county. This storm went on to produce four tornadoes and other significant microburst damage as it continued southeast through Wayne and into Pike county.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of a cold front. Due to cloud cover and limited instability, widespread severe weather did not develop, but isolated storms produced hail across the southern Wyoming Valley.
A series of thunderstorms during the morning of the 13th caused flooding in parts of southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding along the Frankford Creek as well as poor drainage flooding in central Philadelphia. The heavy rain also caused the tracks for the Market-Frankford el to be underwater. Service was suspended between 9 a.m. EDT and 2 p.m. EDT. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 859 a.m. EDT through 1258 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 11.28 feet at 10 a.m. EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding along the Pennypack Creek as well as poor drainage flooding in northeast Philadelphia. A 20-year-old man drowned while rafting on the swollen Pennypack Creek. During the morning, fire officials rescued a man stuck next to the pillar of a rail bridge near the intersection of Ashburner Street and Frankford Avenue on the creek. Later that day, two men fell into the water after their raft overturned near Winchester Avenue and Roosevelt Boulevard, but only one man survived. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1018 a.m. EDT through 617 p.m. EDT EDT on the 13th. It crested at 9.1 feet at 4 p.m. EDT.

The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 910 a.m. EDT through 1031 a.m. EDT EDT on the 13th. It crested at 5.64 feet at 10 a.m. EDT.

As an area of low pressure over Wisconsin moved northeast, a stationary boundary over Pennsylvania pushed northward as a warm front during the morning hours. This put much of northeast Pennsylvania in the warm sector during the day, and scattered  showers and thunderstorms developed later in the day ahead of an approaching cold front and well defined upper level disturbance. A few of the storms that developed produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

In Philadelphia, the heat claimed the life of a 66-year-old woman, a 90-year-old woman (found in an apartment with closed windows and a fan on) and a 56-year-old man. Delaware County opened cooling centers through the weekend of the 24th and 25th.||The heat wave ended with strong to severe thunderstorms that preceded a strong cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 25th. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Philadelphia, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), 95 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in London Grove (Chester County) and 93 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County).  The month of July was one of the hottest Julys on record in Eastern Pennsylvania. It was the 2nd hottest July on record in Philadelphia (monthly mean temperature of 81.7 degrees) and the 6th hottest July on record in Reading (monthly mean temperature of 78.8 degrees). It also tied July 1999 and 1995 for the most days with temperatures of 90 degrees or higher (20 days). Even the Poconos did not escape the heat as it was the third warmest July on record (71.8 degrees) in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

Torrential downpours caused by successive thunderstorms caused flash flooding along the Monocacy Creek as well as urban and poor drainage flooding in the Lehigh Valley during the morning of the 10th. Runoff from morning heavy rain also caused flooding to occur in Chester County along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek on the afternoon of the 10th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 4 to 5 inches in the Lehigh Valley and the Lehigh Valley International Airport measured 5.67 inches. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in central Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the morning heavy rain caused flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek during the afternoon of the 10th. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1225 p.m. EDT through 146 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 7.16 feet at 1 p.m. EDT.

Runoff from morning heavy rain also caused flooding to occur in Chester County along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek on the afternoon of the 10th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 4 to 5 inches in the Lehigh Valley and the Lehigh Valley International Airport measured 5.67 inches. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in central Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of the Monocacy Creek at the Colonial Industrial Quarter in Bethlehem around 8 a.m. EDT on the 10th. The creek flooded four structures in the area near the intersection of Main and West Church Streets. Tree branches slammed against the buildings and a couple of windows were broken. Rainfall reached 5.67 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. The flooding in the area was described as the worst since the remnants of Hurricane Ivan in 2004. Up to about 5 feet of water accumulated within the 1761 Tannery, 1762 Waterworks and 1869 Luckenbach Mill. In the 1762 Waterworks, the 18 foot water wheel was clogged and damaged. In the 1761 Tannery, wooden vats were destroyed, the heating system was damaged and the wooden floor buckled.  There was concern about the 1762 Waterworks and the 1761 Tannery structural integrity. The two buildings took on water for the longest period of time. A few feet of water still occupied the buildings during the afternoon of the 10th. The rushing water also damaged the Springhouse and the ground floor of the 1869 Luckenbach Mill. Elsewhere in the Lehigh Valley, the Schoenersville exit to U.S. Route 22 was closed. Roads were closed due to floodng in Allentown and Bethlehem and basements were flooded in Catasauqua and Lower Nazareth Township. The Monocacy Creek at Bethlehem was above its 5 foot flood stage from 815 a.m. EDT through 1038 a.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 5.44 feet at 9 a.m. EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS meteorologists confirmed a microburst near Muncy in southern Lycoming County around 1640 EDT. Peak thunderstorm winds were estimated near 80 mph based on extensive tree and roof damage. The intense, straight-line downburst winds uprooted or snapped numerous trees, causing structural damage to several homes. One very large tree landed on a vehicle traveling along S. Main Street and resulted in two fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern triggered two minor convective episodes during the mid-morning and late evening hours. The morning severe weather threat was characterized by strongly veered low level wind shear along and north of a warm front that lifted slowly north across the lower Susquehanna Valley. Despite meager instability, rich tropical moisture and the strong low level wind shear fueled fast-moving, shallow rotating convective elements that evolved rapidly from near the Chesapeake Bay north into southeast Pennsylvania. ||The second area of severe weather occurred along and ahead of a strong cold front that pushed through eastern Pennsylvania during the late evening. Weak instability combined with very strong unidirectional winds to produce a few broken lines of severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down numerous trees in Windsor Township near Red Lion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms during the morning of the 13th caused flooding in parts of southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 22nd. A tornado formed along this line in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down in Chester City on the afternoon of the 22nd. The first wind damage occurred near the intersection of 4th and Engle Streets where a couple of trees and a garage was blown down.  A nearly continuous path of tree damage then occurred to the 2000 block of Third Street where several trees were blown down and structural damage occurred to a building and its roof.  Additional damage occurred in a continuous path to the intersection of Pennsylvania State Route 291 and Reaney Street where a house incurred siding and roof damage. The tornado then lifted as it approached the Commodore Barry Bridge where planks were ripped off the new Interstate 95 ramp being constructed. The path length of the tornado was approximately 0.33 miles and its maximum path width was 80 yards. The estimated maximum wind speed was 75 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 5th, the Pennsylvania Department of Conservation issued a drought watch for the Poconos, including Monroe and Carbon Counties. The Department of Conservation urged residents in the Poconos to voluntarily reduce water use by 10 to 15 percent. August concluded the third consecutive month of below normal rainfall in the Poconos for the meteorological summer. On a county weighted average, summer rainfall was 9.0 inches (3.5 inches below normal) in Monroe County and 10.4 inches (2.6 inches below normal) in Carbon County. At Mount Pocono (Monroe County), the summer rainfall total of 8.23 inches was 4.34 inches below the current summer normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was confirmed in Paradise Township, Lancaster County with estimated maximum winds of 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak and very brief EF0 tornado tracked rapidly north along Vintage Road between Route 741 and 30. The tornado lasted approximately 3 to 4 minutes and produced sporadic damage with estimated maximum winds of 85 mph. The damage path was between 20 and 30 yards wide. Several farm buildings sustained significant damage and multiple trees were snapped-off. A commercial roof-top AC unit was overturned and crop damage was visible in nearby corn fields. The area was surveyed by employees from the National Weather Service in State College and Lancaster County Emergency Management. There was one minor injury to a farmer who was inside one of the farm buildings at the time the tornado moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms associated with a stalled cold front moved over eastern Ohio, southwestern Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. These thunderstorms produced damaging straight line winds that caused numerous toppled and snapped trees as well as downed power lines that left an estimated 100 thousand people without power across the region. In Ohio, the hardest hit counties were Harrison and Jefferson, where a tractor trailer was overturned on Route 43. Allegheny and Westmoreland counties in western Pennsylvania were also hit hard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reports trees down in Latrobe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September started with a drought watch in effect for Carbon and Monroe Counties. The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection on September 16th upgraded the Poconos to a drought warning as well as most of the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania except for Chester and Delaware Counties which were placed in a drought watch. A drought warning asks residents to reduce water consumption by 10 to 15 percent. Water suppliers need to monitor supplies and update drought contingency plans. A drought watch calls for a 5 percent reduction in non-essential water use and also puts large water consumers on notice to begin planning for the possibility of reduced water supplies. The Department of Agriculture declared most of Eastern Pennsylvania a drought disaster and made farmers throughout Eastern Pennsylvania eligible to apply for low interest loans to help recover from crop losses. ||A couple of municipalities issued their own warnings. East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) issued a drought warning on September 1st and asked residents to voluntarily cut back on non-essential water use. This was upgraded to a drought emergency on September 20th when its Middle Smithfield Township reservoir fell to about 50 percent of capacity. All non-essential water use from lawn watering to car washing were banned as was the suspension of fire department pump testing and water training, Sellersville (Bucks County) issued a drought warning after its reservoir fell down to one third of capacity and asked residents to cut water usage by ten percent. ||September remained an unseasonably warm month, but precipitation averaged above normal because of the heavy rain at the very end of the month. It was the 5th warmest (72.9 degrees) September on record in Philadelphia and the warm season as a whole established a new record for the number of days that the maximum temperature reached or exceeded 90 degrees (55 days). Reading (Berks County) had the second most ever at 48 days. It was also the 8th warmest (61.5 degrees) September on record in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

A slow moving frontal boundary with waves of low pressure along it produced heavy rain in Pennsylvania on September 30th and October 1st. Remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Nicole was entrained along the frontal boundary. The first wave of heavy rain during the morning of the 30th produced flooding in Chester County. The second wave of heavy rain that started during the evening of the 30th caused more widespread flooding that began late in the evening on September 30th and became more widespread on October 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Valley Creek near Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1236 a.m. EDT through 937 a.m. EDT on October 1st. It crested at 12.83 feet at 445 a.m. EDT on October 1st. The event precipitation total (through October 1st) was 8.25 inches in West Chester..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary with waves of low pressure along it produced heavy rain in Pennsylvania on September 30th and October 1st. Remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Nicole was entrained along the frontal boundary. The first wave of heavy rain during the morning of the 30th produced flooding in Chester County. The second wave of heavy rain that started during the evening of the 30th caused more widespread flooding that began late in the evening on September 30th and became more widespread on October 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first round of heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and small stream flooding during the day on the 30th mainly in the western half of Chester County. In Honeybrook Township, westbound Beaver Dam Road near South Birdell Road flooded. A vehicle stalled in the flood waters and the driver had to be rescued. In Caln Township, the 11th Avenue Bridge was inaccessible. In West Vincent Township, Horseshoe Trail was closed because of flooding between Pennsylvania State Route 113 and Chantilly Road. The Octorara School district dismissed children early because of flooded roads. Two day (including October 1st) storm totals included 9.79 inches in Kennett Square, 8.25 inches in West Chester, 7.49 inches in Nottingham, 7.36 inches in Unionville and 7.27 inches in Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved over the Ohio Valley late on the 16th. After an intial batch of precipitation moved through late morning, a break in the precipitation and cloud cover over east central Ohio allowed for an increase in instability and shear. A line of showers and isolated severe thunderstorms developed and moved across east central Ohio before weakening over western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. A supercell that previously produced a tornado in Holmes county, OH moved into Tuscarawas county, OH where it then produced an EF1 tornado in Dover. Wind damage was also reported in both eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania with other storms as they began to collapse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down on roads in Hempfield Township.
